Second, the more students reported ADHD symptoms, the stronger the positive relationship between teacher emotional and classroom organization (structure and feedback) support and students’ SESR. The findings highlight the importance of perceived teacher support for SESR, especially for students experiencing ADHD symptoms. This finding underscores the importance of teacher support in the classroom in today’s inclusive school practices.

articles.title: Collective Memory Modeling: Utilizing Individualized Representations to Drive Processing Models
articles.authors: Brendan Johns
articles.abstract: This article advances a new framework for computational memory modeling that integrates individual variability in semantic representation with computational models of memory processes. Although existing approaches to computational memory modeling have provided detailed accounts of encoding, retrieval, and decision dynamics, they typically assume fixed representations, overlooking individual differences in linguistic experience. Focusing on false memory in the Deese–Roediger–McDermott (DRM; Roediger & McDermott, 1995) paradigm, individualized distributional semantic models were constructed from social media sources and embedded within the Minerva 2 model of recognition memory while holding process parameters constant. This design isolates the contribution of representational variability to memory performance. Across simulations of 2,000 individual models, substantial variability emerged in item-level predictions. When using an optimized subset of individuals models, aggregated predictions produced strong correspondence with item-level data and exhibited a robust wisdom of the crowd effect (Steyvers & Miller, 2015), where the aggregated item-level data exceeded the performance of the best performing individual model. Additional analyses demonstrated that aggregation stabilizes behavioral predictions while preserving meaningful variability arising from differences in semantic structure. These findings provide an existence proof that representational diversity alone can account for systematic variation in memory behavior and improve predictive accuracy of a proposed model. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of incorporating individualized semantic knowledge into computational models, offering a unified and ecologically valid framework that bridges representation and process in the study of human memory.

articles.title: AI vs. Humans: Does AI Face Harsher Moral Judgments? A Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Olivia Hill-Cousins; Joseph Sweetman; Cassandra J Lowe
articles.abstract:
Research on how people judge AI compared to humans remains fragmented despite its criticality
in resolving a multitude of current AI ethical debates. This pre-registered meta-analysis (OSF:
10.17605/OSF.IO/GUHPQ; PROSPERO: CRD420251237117) addresses this by systematically reviewing
experimental evidence from 50 experimental studies (363 comparisons) comparing moral judgments of
human and AI agents’ decisions in moral dilemmas (e.g. the trolley problem) which were extracted from
PubMed, Web of Science and Elicit. Standardized effect sizes (Hedge’s g) were computed for two-cell
designs (Agent: Human vs. AI). Risk of bias was assessed using a pre-registered checklist for experimental
designs. Results from a three-level random-effects meta-analysis show that AI agents’ moral decisions are
judged as less morally permissible than equivalent human decisions (g = -0.08, p = 0.035). Among several
moderation analyses, perceived mental capacity (PMC) – ascribed through proxies such as agency and
experience – significantly reduced and reversed the negative effect (g = 0.11, p = 0.007). Higher PMC
corresponded to smaller or reversed differences between moral evaluations of human and AI decisions.
Interpretability was constrained by substantial heterogeneity across designs. Together, these findings
reveal asymmetries in the moral frameworks guiding evaluations of human and AI agents and point to
psychological factors that may contribute to variability in human-AI moral expectations. No external
funding.

articles.title: Similar exercise adherence and physical fitness outcomes are observed across distinct motivation profiles in older adults participating in a home-based structured exercise programme
articles.authors: Alexandra Munns; Jack Feron; Joan Duda; Sindre Fosstveit; Kelsey Elizabeth Joyce; Foyzul Rahman; Linda Wheeldon; Hilde Lohne Seiler; Sveinung Berntsen Stølevik; Jet Veldhuijzen van Zanten
articles.abstract:
Purpose:

Motivation and self-efficacy are established predictors of engagement and persistence within structured exercise programmes; however, it remains unclear whether these psychological factors predict objective adherence metrics and associated physical fitness outcomes within home-based exercise programmes for older adults. This study adopted a person-centred approach to examine whether distinct psychological profiles predict adherence and fitness outcomes following a structured home-based exercise programme.

Methods:

Eighty-eight older adults (age = 67.2 ± 5.5 years) completed a 26-week home-based exercise programme. Objective adherence was defined as compliance with prescribed exercise volume and quantified using cumulative metabolic equivalent minutes (MET-mins) derived from heart rate–based measures of exercise intensity and duration. Psychological profiles were identified using latent profile analysis based on autonomous motivation, controlled motivation, and exercise self-efficacy. Cardiorespiratory fitness, muscular strength, flexibility, and mobility were assessed pre- and post-intervention.

Results:

Two psychological profiles were identified: an autonomous and highly confident profile and a moderately autonomous and confident profile. Controlled motivation was comparable between profiles, and no differences were observed in baseline demographic or physiological characteristics. Adherence was high and similar across profiles (e.g., cumulative MET-mins: 129 ± 66% vs. 116 ± 38%). Both profiles demonstrated significant and comparable improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness (V̇O₂peak), muscular strength, flexibility, and mobility.

Conclusions:

Baseline differences in self-efficacy and autonomous motivation did not predict objective adherence or fitness outcomes within a structured, well-supported home-based exercise programme for older adults. Consistent objective monitoring and regular behavioural support may therefore reduce the impact of psychological profile membership on adherence and outcomes.

Keywords: ageing; exercise adherence; home-based exercise; latent profile analysis; autonomous motivation; self-efficacy

articles.title: Designing Minds: Toward Systematic Integration of Psychological Science in Design for Augmented Humanity
articles.authors: Nejra van Zalk
articles.abstract:
Design for augmented humanity increasingly operates at the level of human cognition, emotion, and behaviour. As technologies extend and reconfigure human capabilities, design decisions play a constitutive role in shaping how individuals perceive, think, and act. Despite this, the integration of psychological science into design practice remains fragmented and unsystematic. While psychological concepts are widely referenced, they are often applied in selective and decontextualised ways, limiting both explanatory depth and practical effectiveness.
This paper advances the position that the central challenge is not the absence of psychological science in design, but the lack of its systematic integration. Building on this critique, it introduces a framework of levels of psychological grounding, describing progressive forms of integration from informal awareness and ad hoc application to structured incorporation in design processes, evaluation, and interdisciplinary collaboration. The framework provides a conceptual basis for analysing current practice and guiding more coherent integration.
The paper argues that such integration is essential for designing in the context of augmented humanity, where interventions increasingly influence cognition and behaviour at scale. Systematic psychological integration is therefore positioned as a necessary condition for developing design practices that are not only effective, but also transparent, accountable, and aligned with human well-being.

articles.title: A Tutorial for Navigating Ethical Dilemmas in Research: The Case of Early Language Interventions
articles.authors: Sho Tsuji; Kai Jia Tey; Camila Scaff; Alicia Horton-Mastin; Manel Khentout; Lucas Gautheron; Joseph Coffey; May Abdou; Alejandrina Cristia
articles.abstract: This tutorial paper aims to provide readers with tools to reflect on ethical dilemmas that illustrate how complex decisions can become when scientific goals meet real-world consequences. Through the fictional story of the Unicorn Research Team, we focus on a group of scientists, who find themselves in disagreement with each other regarding whether (or how) to be involved in an early childhood intervention aiming to change caregivers' behaviors. We introduce reflective strategies to help researchers make sense of their own intuitions and understand those of others. First, we show that individuals' views often reflect moral frameworks that incorporate consideration of duty, values and outcomes. Second, we invite readers to consider that moral intuitions emerge from human cognition. Third, we introduce Theory of Change, a method that forces us to clarify what our potential actions realistically can be, what the most important outcomes are, and what is the strength of evidence on the underlying causal chain. Rather than offering answers, this paper encourages researchers to pause, reexamine their instincts, and remain open to others' perspectives, using tools to help oneself, and each other, understand the basis for one's position.

articles.title: Time for Consensus: Open Challenges and Recommendations for Research on Individual Differences in Statistical Learning
articles.authors: Louisa Bogaerts; Davide Crepaldi; Haoyu Zhou; Ana Bautista; Kevin Brown; Li-Yun Wendy Chang; Ruth de Diego-Balaguer; Julia Hofweber; Erin Isbilen; Marc F Joanisse
articles.abstract: Statistical learning (SL), the ability to extract regularities from sensory input, is hypothesized to play a major role in many aspects of higher-level cognition. While earlier investigations centered on group-level effects, a growing body of research now focuses on individual differences in this ability. This consensus paper presents the collective insights of 27 researchers worldwide who are active in the field of individual differences in SL, with the goal of reflecting on the field’s progress so far while outlining key challenges going forward. Our discussion covers possible theoretical and operational definitions of SL, the trade-offs between controlled laboratory tasks and ecologically valid paradigms, considerations in interpreting correlations between SL performance and other cognitive abilities, issues of measurement reliability and statistical power, and rationales for adopting individual-differences versus group-level approaches. Based on these discussions, we outline nine actionable recommendations for the field to move forward, which are meant to lead to a new body of evidence that is more theoretically informed and more methodologically robust.

articles.title: Revealing the Mystery of Emotions in Sounds The Theory of Musical Equilibration Explains the Impact of Ordered Sounds as the Listener’s Identification with Processes of Will
articles.authors: Bernd Willimek; Daniela Willimek
articles.abstract: Science has long been concerned with the question of how it is possible for music to elicit emotions‒so far, however, with only moderate success. The theory of musical equilibration shines a light on this problem from a new perspective. The theory was first proposed in 1997 by German music theorist, Bernd Willimek, who further developed it with his wife and fellow theorist, Daniela Willimek. Music itself, the theory goes, cannot generate emotions directly. Rather, emotions should be understood as a response to the processes of will that are encoded in music. By identifying with these processes of will, listeners experience music emotionally. This is comparable to the situation of moviegoers who identify with the protagonist on screen and thereby vicariously experience emotions. In this work, the affirmative character of the major triad and the negative character of the minor triad are substantiated. According to the theory of musical equilibration, the volume at which a minor chord is played determines whether it is perceived as sorrow or anger. Furthermore, the authors discuss issues such as why a diminished seventh chord is well suited as the score for film scenes involving fear, or how an augmented chord can convey amazement and astonishment. To show the practical application of the theory of musical equilibration, the authors carried out tests with school (K-12) and university students (basic test part A, basic test part B, Rocky test).

articles.title: From Human to AI: How Delegation to AI Shifts Moral Responsibility Attribution
articles.authors: Philippe Sloksnath
articles.abstract:
As AI systems increasingly make moral decisions on behalf of humans, questions arise
about how this delegation reshapes moral responsibility. Yet little is known about
whether people hold AI systems responsible for delegated moral decisions, and whether
this differs from delegating to human agents. This study examines people’s willingness
to delegate moral decisions to AI systems or human agents, and how delegation relates
to the attribution of moral responsibility. Across two studies and six moral dilemmas (N =
669), the effects of agent type (human vs. AI), domain (business vs. healthcare), and
trade-off severity (low vs. high) on delegation and responsibility attribution were
examined. Results show no evidence that delegation differed between AI systems and
humans. When decision stakes are high, delegation increases. Furthermore, delegation
is associated with a partial transfer of perceived moral responsibility toward the
delegate, to both AI systems and humans, resulting in a mostly shared responsibility
pattern between delegator and delegate. The findings challenge machine aversion
assumptions and reveal a psychological disposition to attribute responsibility to AI
systems.

articles.title: Dimensions of Victimhood
articles.authors: Emily Kubin; Kurt Gray
articles.abstract: Moral and political conflicts revolve around victimhood —who is harmed, how severely, and even whether harm has occurred at all. Although humans have always cared about concrete interpersonal victimhood, the complexity of modern society creates many varieties of victimhood, which we organize along two dimensions: Scale (the number of victims) and Tangibility (the concreteness of victimization). These dimensions exist in both the mind and the world and are grounded in domain-general cognitive processes: epistemic trust and numerical cognition (scale), and ontological assumptions and linguistic scaffolding (tangibility). These dimensions help explain elements of political dynamics from polarization to misinformation, help map moral cognition to cognitive science, and suggest multiple directions for future research.

articles.title: Audiovisual Entertainment Media Narratives and Openness toward Underrepresented Groups: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Joël Hendrix; Gijsbert Bijlstra; Rebecca de Leeuw; Wieke M. Knol; Marieke Fransen
articles.abstract: Research examining the relationship between exposure to audiovisual entertainment media narratives and openness toward underrepresented groups has been conducted across multiple disciplines, resulting in high heterogeneity in methods and fragmented findings. This systematic review synthesizes empirical studies (N = 104) across disciplines to examine this relationship and its underlying mechanisms. A comprehensive search yielded studies examining affective, cognitive, and behavioral dimensions of openness. Our synthesis revealed largely inconclusive findings across these dimensions. However, we found most evidence for associations with or effects on reduced prejudice and aversion outcomes. Additionally, we found the most conclusive evidence for outgroup identification as an underlying mechanism. The underrepresented groups for whom we found most evidence of relationships and effects were people with disabilities, disorders, and mental illness. While audiovisual entertainment media narratives can positively influence openness toward underrepresented groups, the mechanisms and conditions under which these effects occur require further investigation. Recommendations for research practices are discussed to advance understanding of entertainment media narratives in promoting openness toward underrepresented groups.

articles.title: The FRIEND Procedure: A Fast-Friends Paradigm With Nonviolent Communication Priming for Dyadic Belonging Research
articles.authors: Vince Jacques de Blauwe; Antonio Fidalgo
articles.abstract: The Fast Friends Procedure (FFP) is a widely used paradigm for generating interpersonal closeness in stranger dyads, but standard versions specify the disclosure prompts far more clearly than they specify how participants are prepared for reciprocal vulnerability (Aron et al., 1997; Sprecher, 2021). This design note documents the FRIEND procedure: a feasibility-stage adaptation of the FFP that introduced a brief Nonviolent Communication (NVC) primer immediately before the dyadic task, paired with the development of a provisional companion measure, the Dyadic Engagement and Safety Index (DESI-14) (Rosenberg, 2003). The study ran at the University of East London with 30 participants forming 15 stranger dyads, randomised to either a 15-minute NVC primer or a duration-matched body-scan mindfulness control delivered immediately before the FFP. This note documents the FRIEND protocol in enough detail for methodological reuse. It also traces how DESI-14 emerged from an earlier DESI-12 pilot rather than presenting the instrument as a finished product without lineage. The procedure was deliverable within a single laboratory session and generated a medium between-group Inclusion of Other in the Self (IOS) signal favouring the NVC condition (U = 160.5, p = .041, rank-biserial r = .43), though a complete-case analysis reduced this to p = .058, rendering it statistically fragile and the feasibility sample was not designed for confirmatory efficacy inference (Aron et al., 1992). Pilot evidence from DESI-12 suggested ceiling-prone items and under-specified coverage of inclusion, reciprocal attunement, and non-verbal safety signalling. Three items were removed and five new items added, yielding DESI-14 as a trial-stage instrument under active development rather than a completed psychometric product. The article contributes a replicable dyadic protocol and a transparent developmental record for a still-emerging emotional-safety measure.

articles.title: Measuring Artificial Intelligence Literacy: A Systematic Review of Instrument Development, Conceptual Foundations, and Psychometric Quality
articles.authors: Yueqiao Jin; Dragan Gašević; Roberto Martinez-Maldonado; Lixiang Yan
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) literacy is rapidly emerging as a defining competence of contemporary digital life, yet the ways in which it is measured remain fragmented and methodologically uneven. This study presents a systematic review of AI literacy measurement instruments, synthesising their conceptual foundations, development procedures, and psychometric evidence. Following the PRISMA protocol and guided by the COSMIN framework, we analysed 58 studies covering 47 unique AI literacy instruments published between 2014 and December 2025. The review mapped the measurement landscape, examined how AI literacy is conceptualised and operationalised, and evaluated evidence for content validity and other measurement properties. The results show that instrument development has accelerated sharply since 2023, coinciding with the widespread diffusion of generative AI technologies across educational and professional contexts. Conceptually, most instruments converge on a multidimensional core encompassing AI knowledge, ethical awareness, and practical engagement with AI systems. Methodologically, however, the evidence base remains uneven: while structural validity and internal consistency are frequently reported, evidence for content validity, criterion validity, and cross-cultural measurement invariance remains limited. Moreover, the field is dominated by self-report questionnaires, with relatively few performance-based or process-oriented assessments. These findings indicate that AI literacy measurement has expanded faster than its methodological consolidation. The review highlights the need for theoretically grounded, context-sensitive, and process-oriented approaches, potentially integrating learning analytics and longitudinal designs, to capture how individuals actually develop and enact AI literacy in increasingly AI-mediated learning environments.

articles.title: Predicting Multilevel Growth Trajectories Using a Random-Effect Diagnostic Classification Model
articles.authors: Kazuhiro Yamaguchi; Haruhiko Mitsunaga; Shun Saso; Yuri Uesaka
articles.abstract: Learning diagnosis is essential for effective education, with formative assessments shown to significantly enhance academic performance. Diagnostic classification models have been developed to assess students' learning status and provide remedial instruction. However, the impact of mastery or non-mastery of specific attributes on long-term learning development remains uncertain. If certain non-mastered attributes hinder the growth of mathematical ability, early intervention becomes essential. In this study, we developed a random-effects diagnostic classification model for multilevel growth curves (RDC–MGC) model to identify the specific effects of attribute mastery on individual-level mathematics ability growth. The simulation studies showed that the Bayesian estimation procedure provided appropriate parameter recovery and coverage probabilities, whereas ignoring the multilevel structure resulted in biased parameter estimates. The model was applied to arithmetic test data from second- to sixth-grade elementary school students. Diagnosis was conducted in the second grade, and the effects of mastery on mathematics ability growth from the third to sixth grades were assessed. The results showed that attribute mastery in second grade was associated with both the intercept and slope of individual ability growth, suggesting the potential importance of early-stage diagnostic information for understanding later mathematical development. Potential extensions of the proposed RDC–MGC model are also discussed.

articles.title: Linking behavioral and neural measures of visual search
articles.authors: Zoe (Jing) Xu; Kristin M. Woodard; Brenda Qiu; Bridget Leonard; Scott O. Murray
articles.abstract: Visual search is a ubiquitous behavior, yet its neural mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Here, we combine behavioral modeling and fMRI to identify neural signatures of core visual search computations. Participants performed visual search tasks in which targets differed from distractors along color, shape, or both dimensions, while attentional focus was experimentally manipulated. We show that behavioral search slopes – quantifying how response time scales with set size – are mirrored by corresponding fMRI-derived slopes, such that neural responses increase systematically with set size and scale with target-distractor similarity. This correspondence identifies a neural index of search difficulty and implicates regions in mid- and higher-level visual cortex in target-template matching processes. In addition, we replicate behavioral evidence for flexible attentional weighting of feature dimensions and demonstrate that this prioritization is reflected in modulation of neural activity in a subset of higher-level visual regions. Finally, we show that fMRI responses in bidimensional search can be predicted from activity measured during unidimensional search, demonstrating that computational models of behavior generalize to neural data. Together, these findings establish a quantitative link between behavioral and neural measures of visual search and provide a framework for studying attentional guidance and feature integration in the human brain.

articles.title: The Psychological, Emotional, and Relational Impact of Non-Suicidal Self-Injury on Parents: A Meta-Analysis Protocol
articles.authors: Kassandra Hon; Penelope Hasking; Stephen P Lewis; Katrina Hon; Mark Edward Boyes; Jennifer J Muehlenkamp
articles.abstract: Objective: This meta-analysis aims to systematically synthesise the evidence on the psychological, emotional, and relational experiences of parents of individuals who engage in non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI). Introduction: NSSI is a significant public health concern associated with adverse outcomes for both individuals and their families. While a substantial body of research has examined NSSI among young people, less attention has been given to its impact on parents and caregivers. Existing studies reveal that parents often experience considerable psychological distress, emotional burden, and disruption to family functioning following awareness of their child’s NSSI. However, a quantitative synthesis is needed to clarify the magnitude and direction of these parental outcomes and to identify gaps in the current evidence base to inform future research and the development of targeted supports for parents. Inclusion criteria: The review will include empirical studies examining parents or caregivers of individuals who engage in NSSI. Eligible studies must report at least one outcome related to parental psychological, emotional, or relational experiences. Methods: The review will follow the PRISMA guideline for meta-analyses. Electronic searches will be conducted across Medline, Scopus, ProQuest, and Web of Science. Two reviewers will independently screen studies, extract data, and assess risk of bias using standardised tools. Pooled effect sizes and random effect models will be calculated using meta-analysis. Results and Conclusion: This meta-analysis will provide a synthesis of the psychological, emotional, and relational experiences of parents of individuals who engage in NSSI and identify gaps in the literature. Findings will inform future research and support the development of evidence-informed interventions and supports for parents and caregivers of individuals who self-injure.

articles.title: Age and Gender Differences in Body Talk Frequency and Longitudinal Associations Between Body Talk and Body Image Variables
articles.authors: Nahori Ito; Akira Hasegawa
articles.abstract: The first purpose of this study was to investigate age and gender differences in three types of body talk: negative fat talk, negative muscle talk, and positive body talk. The second purpose was to examine longitudinal associations between body talk and body image variables at the within-person level, while distinguishing them from between-person associations. In this three-wave online study, a total of 5,707 women and men aged 15–94 years participated in the Wave 1 survey, and 3,597 completed two follow-up surveys administered at two-month intervals. Participants completed measures of body talk, body fat dissatisfaction, drive for muscularity, body appreciation, and the internalization of thin and athletic body ideals at each wave. Analyses of Wave 1 data indicated that negative fat talk was characteristic of women in their teens and twenties, whereas negative muscle talk was characteristic of men in the same age groups. Random-intercept cross-lagged panel model analyses of the three-wave data revealed that most longitudinal associations between body talk and body image variables at the within-person level were not statistically significant across age and gender groups. In contrast, significant between-person associations were observed across age groups in both women and men, including positive correlations between negative fat talk and body fat dissatisfaction and between positive body talk and body appreciation. These longitudinal findings suggest that previously reported associations between body talk and body image variables may largely reflect relatively stable individual differences, with less clear evidence for a mutually reinforcing relationship between body talk and body image variables.

articles.title: Learning in context: Socioemotional stimuli enhance cognitive learning processes but not psychophysiological engagement in early adolescence
articles.authors: Meriah Lee DeJoseph; Kathleen M. Thomas; Willem Frankenhuis; Daniel Berry
articles.abstract: Throughout development, we learn through trial and error in the face of uncertainty. This ‘reinforcement learning’ supports youth’s ability to adapt to environmental demands. Theory suggests that early experiences optimize learning to prioritize socially relevant cues across multiple levels of analysis. Yet, experimental paradigms often use abstract stimuli and limit measures of learning to behavioral summaries such as task accuracy. This study uses computational models grounded in reinforcement learning and dynamic systems frameworks to examine how socioemotional context modulates cognitive and psychophysiological learning processes. In a sample of 56 youth aged 12-15 years, a within-person experimental design quantified continuous behavior and heart rate (~700 observations of behavior and heart rate, per person) during an adapted reinforcement learning task with stimuli that varied in socioemotional relevance. A series of Bayesian multilevel models revealed that, compared to traditionally-used non-emotional stimuli, learning from stimuli high in socioemotional arousal enhanced behavioral performance. Specifically, computational models suggested that socioemotional salience may elicit faster value-updating processes and a qualitative shift to more exploitative decision-making. Underlying psychophysiological engagement was modulated by heightened sensitivity to learning from rewards, showing faster value-updating in the context of rewards aligned with more optimal psychophysiological flexibility and organization. Together, these findings clarify the contexts and modulatory processes that shape developmental learning mechanisms.

articles.title: “Unbrake my rejected heart”: Cardiac slowing when maltreated youth reconnect with peers who resemble caregivers
articles.authors: Dorukhan Açıl; Jan Keil; Angelika Bracher; Pascal Vrticka; Tobias Stalder; Nina Alexander; Michael Crowley; Kai von Klitzing; Lars O. White
articles.abstract: Adverse caregiving experiences are thought to bias internal representations of self and others, giving rise to negative expectations that generalize to new encounters. However, few studies examine whether cues signaling resemblance to caregivers selectively increase the odds of generalization. In this preregistered study, 133 participants were assessed for maltreatment at two waves (n = 65 maltreated; ages 4–16 and 11–24 years). At the second wave, participants played Cyberball. Following social exclusion, participants re-engaged with two novel co-players, who ostensibly introduced themselves via self-descriptive blurbs: The Target implicitly resembled the participant’s caregiver, whereas the Control resembled someone else’s caregiver. The co-players threw the ball to participants (re-inclusions) or not (re-exclusions) at equal rates. We assessed event-related cardiac slowing, a correlate of expectancy violation. Cardiac slowing to Target was most pronounced in younger participants and declined with age, suggesting that caregiver cues exert a stronger influence on peer interactions at younger ages. Supporting our pre-registered hypotheses, during re-exclusions, maltreated participants showed greater cardiac slowing to Control vs. Target, whereas nonmaltreated participants exhibited the opposite pattern. We conclude that, for maltreated individuals, re-exclusion violates expectancies less when stemming from caregiver-like targets, implying that resemblance cues trigger selective generalization during early psychophysiological processing.

articles.title: Scalable estimation of continuous perceptual representations from human judgements and computer vision: An application to melanoma identification
articles.authors: Murray Scott Bennett; Joseph Woodworth Houpt; James T. Townsend; Michael Wenger; Lawrence Mark
articles.abstract: Human perceptual judgements are central to many classification and decision tasks, yet they are often measured using coarse, discrete ratings that obscure the continuous structure of underlying perceptual representations. We present a scalable measurement framework for recovering continuous, ratio-scale perceptual representations from pairwise human judgements using the Bradley-Terry-Luce (BTL) model. We apply this framework to a large natural image set (10,000 skin lesion images) to derive interpretable perceptual representations of dimensions central to melanoma risk assessment: shape asymmetry, border irregularity, and colour variance. We evaluate the face validity and reliability of these perceptual measures and compare them to deterministic computer vision estimates of the same features. The resulting representations show moderate correspondence, indicating partial overlap but substantial non-redundant information. We demonstrate the functional utility of these measurements by evaluating classification performance using models trained on human-derived perceptual measures, computer vision estimates, and their combination. We found that integrating human and computational representations yields improved diagnostic discrimination relative to either source alone, illustrating representational complementarity. The proposed pairwise comparison and modelling approach we present enables efficient, scalable recovery of continuous perceptual representations for large stimulus sets and provides a principled framework for comparing representations across human and machine systems.

articles.title: Aloneness and Identity Across the Lifespan
articles.authors: Rowena Leary; Kathryn Asbury
articles.abstract: This study considered how attitudes towards being alone develop across the lifespan and whether and how they differ between generations.  Drawing on Erikson’s theory of identity development we asked whether developing aloneness attitudes might interlink with identity development, and whether having a strong sense of integrated identity may promote positive attitudes about aloneness. We asked 95 adult participants to share qualitative stories about their experiences of being alone across the lifespan. Analysis of their stories, using framework analysis, allowed for comparison across generations. We developed three themes: 1) Identity integration in youth supports positive attitudes towards aloneness across the lifespan; 2) Unstable or lacking identity integration may be linked to acute loneliness in middle age; and 3) Active self-reflection promotes identity integration and protects against loneliness across generations. These findings suggest that identity development, particularly in early life, may be important to the promotion of a positive attitude towards aloneness and may protect against loneliness across the lifespan. The benefits of self-reflection are also highlighted.

articles.title: Spiritual Patience: Preliminary Results
articles.authors: Allison Alford; Madelyn Chance; Anne Jeffrey; Devan Stahl; Sarah Schnitker
articles.abstract: In these preliminary results, we develop and validate a measure of spiritual patience. Spiritual patience is defined as the virtue of abiding well (not poorly) with transcendent silence, absence, or obscurity, motivated by a longing and love for the divine or a transcendent force. In Studies 1 and 2, spiritual patience is revealed to be a one factor, 7-item scale and excellent internal consistency. In Study 3, our measure of spiritual patience demonstrated good internal consistency, discriminant validity, convergent validity, and incremental validity. In Study 4, spiritual patience was found to mediate the relations between spiritual yearning and meaning in life as well as depression. Overall, these results put forward a sound measure of spiritual patience and demonstrate this virtue's importance in spiritual flourishing.

articles.title: Nothing “Evaluative“ about Evaluative Priming?  Semantic Relatedness Beyond Evaluation Is Not Necessary for a Facilitation of Evaluative Judgements
articles.authors: Manuel Becker; Marie Jakob; Karl Christoph Klauer
articles.abstract: Ever since Zajonc proposed that preferences need no inferences, psychologists have debated whether evaluations can be conceptualized and measured independently of co-activated semantic content. One prominent arena for this debate has been the Evaluative Priming Task, which tracks the effects of conflicting or congruent evaluative relationships between stimuli. Surprisingly, two prior lines of research found that effects of evaluative congruency in the task do not emerge in the absence of additional semantic relationships. This not only conflicts with other findings, but also challenges dominant theoretical accounts of the task, whose assumptions entail that congruency effects should arise irrespectively. In Studies 1 and 2 (Ns=81, 85), we re-examine these two lines of research and show that when vulnerabilities in the original experimental and analytical designs are rectified, evaluative congruency effects do reliably occur (even in the absence of strong semantic relationships). A third study (N=80), that implements strong semantic relationships under especially controlled conditions, also yields reliable congruency effects. In Study 4 (N=81), the trials with low semantic relatedness from the first three studies are combined, replicating consistent and reliable, but small congruency effects. Our results thereby strengthen models that accommodate an independent role of evaluations, and we illustrate how these models might be modified to account for an augmenting influence of semantic relatedness. Currently, in domains for which evaluations and their measurement are integral (like the measurement of race-based evaluations, consumer or health behavior), the pronounced and potentially biasing effects of semantic relatedness remain largely unrecognized as a serious confound.

articles.title: Dissociating behavioral, neural and experiential effects of prefrontal HD-tDCS during conflict resolution
articles.authors: Darias Holgado; Víctor Martínez-Pérez; Elisa Martín-Arévalo; Tristan Bekinschtein; Miguel A. Fernández-del-Olmo; Daniel Sanabria; Antonio Luque-Casado
articles.abstract: Inconsistent evidence regarding the cognitive effects of transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) highlights the need for more comprehensive approaches to assess its impact. This study aimed to investigate the effects of high-definition tDCS (HD-tDCS) on conflict resolution by combining behavioral, neural, and subjective experience measures. Sixty participants were randomly assigned to anodal, cathodal, or sham HD-tDCS groups and completed a 30-minute flanker task. EEG was recorded during the first and last blocks (without stimulation), while stimulation was applied during the intermediate blocks of the task. Using a multidimensional methodological approach including Drift-Diffusion Modeling (DDM), EEG spectral analysis, Lempel-Ziv complexity, and Temporal Experience Tracing (TET), we assessed the cognitive, neural, and phenomenological effects of stimulation. Behavioral results indicated no significant improvements in reaction times or accuracy across the stimulation groups. Similarly, DDM parameters showed no effect of HD-tDCS on latent cognitive processes. However, EEG data revealed a significant reduction in neural complexity in the anodal group during resting-state, suggesting a stabilization or reorganization of neural dynamics. Subjective experience analysis identified two distinct clusters of task-related feelings, though time spent in these experiential states did not differ between groups. Interestingly, sensation of stimulation was significantly higher for anodal stimulation than sham when analyzed as a single dimension. Despite null behavioral effects, this study provides important insights into the neural and subjective responses to HD-tDCS and highlights the value of integrating complementary multidimensional approaches to better characterize brain stimulation effects. These findings contribute to the ongoing debate about the efficacy of tDCS in cognitive enhancement.

articles.title: Exploring the Lower-Order Structure of Trait Disinhibition
articles.authors: Nathaniel Luke Phillips; Donald R Lynam; Ashley Langford; Leigha Rose; Kaela Van Til; Brinkley M. Sharpe; Josh Miller
articles.abstract: Disinhibition is a core domain across models of personality and psychopathology, and it is robustly associated with many important outcomes. Disagreement remains concerning its lower-order structure, as existing models and measures differ in the number, content, and breadth of its underlying facets. This preregistered study examined the hierarchical structure of Disinhibition as assessed via self-report pathological personality measures using item-level analyses. A pool of Disinhibition-related items (138 items) was assessed using Goldberg’s “bass-ackward” factor-analytic (2006) procedure in an undergraduate sample (N = 929). Results supported a hierarchical four-factor solution comprising Rashness, Unreliability, Risk Taking, and Distractibility. These factors showed strong convergence with PID-5 and CAT-PD Disinhibition, relatively distinct profiles of association with varying personality traits, and meaningful relations with externalizing and internalizing criteria. Content reflecting overcontrol (e.g., perfectionism) was included in the pool but was not featured in the derived structure. Implications for models and measurement of Disinhibition are discussed.

articles.title: Parsing Causal Relations in Social Science Publications
articles.authors: Rasoul Norouzi; Bennett Kleinberg; Jeroen Vermunt; Caspar J. Van Lissa
articles.abstract: Systematically cataloging causal claims made in the social sciences and parsing them into structured cause-effect relations is vital for accumulating knowledge and testing theories across large bodies of literature. Manual review is infeasible due to the scale of the literature and linguistic ambiguity, while existing automated methods often lack domain specificity or fail to extract specific cause-effect pairs. We introduce a BERT-based multitask deep learning model trained on a novel, domain-specific benchmark dataset of 3,014 annotated sentences from social science papers. The model simultaneously (1) identifies sentences containing causal claims, (2) parses them by extracting cause and effect spans (including mediating variables via a new annotation schema), and (3) links the associated causal pairs. The model achieved a reliability level matching that of human coders (Krippendorff’s α = 0.80) and robust F1 performance across configurations. The model and dataset are available as validated, open-source resources that offer a scalable, transparent, and reproducible alternative to manual annotation. It can be used to automatically catalogue causal claims, thereby contributing to research synthesis and theory development.

articles.title: Bias Feedback as Moral Threat: Shame, Defensiveness, and Short-Term Self-Concept Change
articles.authors: Jonathan Doriscar; Wendi L. Gardner; Sylvia Perry
articles.abstract: Bias feedback indicating that one's responses may reflect racial bias despite egalitarian intentions can function as a moral threat to the self. Across three studies and four samples (N = 1,266), we tested whether high (vs. low) bias feedback elicits shame and defensiveness, whether bias awareness shapes those responses, and how shame and defensiveness relate to self-concept clarity, moral self-image, and self-esteem after feedback. High (vs. low) bias feedback increased both shame and defensiveness. Bias awareness was positively associated with shame and lower defensiveness, though effects varied across studies. Shame was most consistently associated with lower self-concept clarity, moral self-image, and self-esteem, indicating disruption in both self-understanding and self-evaluation. Defensiveness followed a narrower pattern in Studies 2 and 3, linked to relatively preserved self-concept clarity alongside lower moral self-image and self-esteem. Shame and defensiveness appear to be distinct responses to bias feedback with different short-term consequences for self-understanding and self-evaluation.

articles.title: A systematic review of factors associated with adherence to prophylaxis during chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear emergencies
articles.authors: Alex F. Martin; Nikki Heinze; Madeline Victore Stein; Richard Amlôt; James Rubin
articles.abstract: Post-exposure prophylaxis may be recommended during chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear (CBRN) emergencies and must often be initiated rapidly to be effective. Understanding factors that affect uptake (initiation) and subsequent adherence (completion) will help to minimise barriers and support adherence during emergencies. This systematic review synthesises evidence on factors associated with uptake of and adherence to prophylaxis following exposure to anthrax, radiation, smallpox, or viral pandemic diseases such as influenza or COVID-19. The review was pre-registered (https://osf.io/f5nmw). We searched Medline, Embase, APA PsycINFO, Web of Science, Scopus, the Cochrane Library, and subject specific databases, with no language or date restrictions. PRISMA and synthesis without meta-analysis (SWiM) guidelines were followed alongside NICE recommendations for risk of bias assessment. Data were extracted on factors associated with, and interventions targeting, uptake and adherence to prophylaxis across the four scenarios. Of 17,217 records screened, 21 studies were included. Uptake and adherence varied widely. Evidence on associated factors was inconsistent and generally of low certainty. Few scenario-specific differences were identified; variation was more strongly associated with population and contextual factors. Common barriers included concerns about side effects, low perceived risk, and lack of trust in information sources. Evidence on the effectiveness of interventions was limited to two studies, at high risk of bias. In conclusion, preparedness should prioritise addressing shared motivational and practical barriers across settings. There is a critical need to develop and evaluate interventions to support timely uptake and adherence to prophylaxis before emergencies occur.

articles.title: Humanlike AI May Strengthen Women’s Belief in Sexist Stereotypes
articles.authors: Sterling Williams-Ceci; Rachel Minkowitz; Lior Zalmanson; Michael MACY; Mor Naaman
articles.abstract: Can interacting with humanlike AI strengthen sexist stereotype beliefs in vulnerable groups? AI models are frequently repeating gender stereotypes, and anthropomorphic features have been shown to increase individuals’ perceptions of AI’s trustworthiness. Although classic theories have focused on stereotype formation instead of moderation – and traditional social cognition research has suggested that stereotypes lack malleability – we raise the concern that anthropomorphic AI may further strengthen stereotypes in individuals who have formed them. Consistent with this prediction, we report results from four experiments (N = 2,774) showing that politically conservative women believed the gender-math stereotype in a chatbot's response to be more accurate when the chatbot had lifelike features. The effect was mediated by perceived anthropomorphism (specifically, mind perception) and trustworthiness, and we ruled out an alternative cognitive mechanism. Neither liberal women nor conservative men showed this effect; however, liberal women were indirectly influenced in the same way when the humanlike chatbot responded with a different gender stereotype that liberals have formed. In a formal model, we speculate that ideological predisposition and social identity threat may converge, making women more susceptible to believing ideologically-aligned gender stereotypes when humanlike AI asserts them. Our research shows that women’s beliefs in gender stereotypes may be influenced by brief interactions with AI, which extends the traditional theoretical focus on initial formation of these biases and challenges past views of post-formation stereotypes as immutable. Future research should test whether this effect occurs for other identity groups with stereotypes they are predisposed to believe.

articles.title: Using LLMs to Evaluate the Interplay Between PTSD Symptom Severity and Intolerance to Uncertainty: A Large-scale EMA Study
articles.authors: Elisa Xu; Scott Feltman; Siddharth Mangalik; Whitney R. Ringwald; Roman Kotov; Aprajita Mohanty; H. Andrew Schwartz
articles.abstract: Intolerance of uncertainty (IU) is central to cognitive models of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), reflecting a maladaptive process that maintains hypervigilant threat appraisals and symptom persistence. Traditional IU self-report assessments, however, are constrained to explicit, trait-like evaluations and may not capture IU’s implicit and context-dependent nature. Ecological momentary assessment (EMA) combined with large language models (LLMs) offers a scalable approach for detecting subtle linguistic markers of IU in daily life. Here, we introduce a novel language-based method for monitoring IU through naturalistic daily diaries and examine its role in perpetuating PTSD symptoms. In a 90-day EMA study of 432 trauma-exposed individuals with elevated PTSD symptoms (29,623 diary entries with 32,941 daily PTSD symptom ratings), we developed an LLM-based IU assessment, yielding the LLM-based similarity between daily transcribed speech and established IU scale language (r = .60 between LLM measure and human ratings). Using dynamic structural equation modeling (DSEM), we found that higher daily IU predicted greater next-day PTSD symptom severity (β = 0.041, 95% CI [0.024, 0.056]), even after controlling for prior-day symptoms. Conversely, higher daily PTSD symptom severity predicted greater next-day IU (β = 0.035, 95% CI [0.018, 0.054]). The strength of these bidirectional effects varied across individuals (IU→PTSD variance = 0.014, 95% CI [0.007, 0.022]; PTSD→IU variance = 0.037, 95% CI [0.030, 0.047]), reflecting substantial heterogeneity in daily IU–PTSD dynamics. Finally, individuals with stronger IU persistence (IUₜ₋₁ → IUₜ) tended to exhibit enhanced same-day IU–PTSD associations (IUₜ ↔ PCLₜ; r = 0.23, 95% CI = [0.016, 0.410]). In contrast, greater PTSD persistence (PCLₜ₋₁ → PCLₜ) covaried negatively with both lagged (IUₜ₋₁ → PCLₜ; r = -0.38, 95% CI = [-0.505, -0.246]) and contemporaneous (IUₜ ↔ PCLₜ; r = -0.18, 95% CI = [-0.327, -0.024]) IU–PTSD effects, suggesting divergent dynamic patterns relative to IU. Complementary multilevel analyses demonstrated that language-derived IU provides incremental validity, explaining variance in PTSD symptom severity beyond sentiment (NRCSent) and positive and negative affect (LIWC). These findings extend cognitive models of PTSD with IU as a dynamic mechanism influencing symptom trajectories in daily life and demonstrate an ecologically valid framework for tracking implicit cognitive processes via natural language.

articles.title: Commentary: How to Describe Great Ape Personality. Confusion, Problems, and   Errors in Minkov (2026) "The Human Five Factor Personality Model Is   Not Appropriate for Describing Great Ape Personality"
articles.authors: Alexander Weiss
articles.abstract: Minkov (2026) claimed that great ape personality is not well-described by the five personality dimensions found in humans, those being Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness to Experience, Agreeableness, and Conscientiousness. To support his claim, the paper's author described analyses in which he examined personality factor structures obtained from studies of great apes that assessed personality by means of rating scales. Specifically, the author tested for configural invariance by computing the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor as in humans in chimpanzees, orangutans, mountain gorillas, and bonobos, and the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor across multiple studies of chimpanzees. The author also discussed the results of studies that used other measures to assess personality in primates. The author concluded by proposing an alternative approach to studying personality in great apes. In this commentary, I first show that the author mischaracterized the personality research program embraced by many comparative psychologists. I then review the evidence the author uses to support his claim of low invariance, highlighting problems and errors in their analyses and reporting of results. Finally, I show that the author did not take due care when reviewing the literature. I conclude by dispelling a common misunderstanding about comparative research on personality in great apes and other animals.

articles.title: Disentangling Within- and Between-Subject Correlations in Cognitive Models: The Essential Role of Hierarchical Estimation
articles.authors: Michelle C. Donzallaz; Niek Stevenson; Andrew Heathcote; Dora Matzke
articles.abstract: Cognitive models, such as evidence-accumulation models, are increasingly used in individual differences research in psychology and neuroscience. By computing correlations between cognitive model parameters across participants, researchers aim to understand how the psychological processes the parameters represent relate to one another and jointly determine performance. It is generally acknowledged that cognitive models can be challenging to estimate due to strong within-subject correlations among the parameters, which are embedded in the model's likelihood function. What is less often recognized, however, is that within-subject correlations can also distort correlations computed between parameters estimated with non-hierarchical methods, so they no longer reflect true individual differences, potentially leading to misleading conclusions. Here we illustrate this pitfall of non-hierarchical estimation and show how appropriately parameterized, descriptively adequate hierarchical models can mitigate the problem by effectively separating within- and between-subject sources of variation. We then offer recommendations for identifying and guarding against the inferential biases resulting from the strong within-subject correlations inherent in many cognitive models.

articles.title: Aesthetic Emotions and the Drive for Knowledge
articles.authors: Joerg Fingerhut
articles.abstract:
In this paper, I address how the arts contribute to knowledge and understanding by defending an Affective Aesthetic Cognitivism (AAC). AAC is an empirically informed theory model, concerning the role of affect and emotions in sustaining the transformative, cognitive impact of the arts.  The relation of our art engagement to our drive for knowledge might at times seem rather indirect. We do not mainly engage with art for educational purposes but rather for pleasure (e.g., when we rewatch a movie or TV show we love) or for emotion regulation (e.g., when we go to an art exhibition when we are bored). Aesthetic Cognitivism in philosophy and psychology, in contrast, focuses on the epistemic outcomes of our art engagement. It provides a theory about the value of the arts not “as sources of delight, amusement, pleasure, or emotional catharsis, but as sources of understanding.” (Brewer 2025, 1). The proposed AAC in this chapter goes one step further by integrating affect and cognition, arguing that the emotions are not a byproduct but a significant driver of the epistemic impact of the arts. The claim of AAC is therefore not just that the emotions accompany epistemic changes, or that they motivate cognitive engagement (they do that as well), but that specific aesthetic emotions play a constitutive role in how understanding through the arts is achieved. 
	I first discuss the more general impact of art and cultural artifacts on our habits of perceiving and distinguish them from epistemically valuable cognitive gains; this gives me the opportunity to revisit some central claims of Aesthetic Cognitivism. I then examine how affect and cognition have been related in aesthetic psychology and, starting with Berlyne’s (1960, 1970) work on curiosity that recent psychology has been amended to cover more specific emotions. The main part of the chapter analyzes two recent empirical approaches to Aesthetic Cognitivism. An attempt at a taxonomy of aesthetic impacts (Christensen et al., 2023) and an assessment of viewers’ affective and cognitive experiences in a museum context (Miller et al., 2025). I propose some conditions that empirical studies must meet to speak to Aesthetic Cognitivism and AAC respectively. Overall, these studies suggest that when people actively engage with artworks their emotional involvement is not merely reactive or pleasure-based, but contains different expansive experiences that closely connect to shifts in perspective and the generation of understanding.

articles.title: Stability and Change of Social and Emotional Loneliness Across the Life Span: Evidence From Two Large-Scale Longitudinal Studies
articles.authors: Anne Neuber; Naemi Brandt; Susanne Buecker
articles.abstract: Loneliness is a multidimensional experience comprising social and emotional facets. While previous research shows that loneliness exhibits both stable and dynamic patterns across the life span, little is known about how these trajectories unfold at the facet-level. The present study investigates stability and change in social and emotional loneliness across the adult life span using two longitudinal, nationally representative datasets from the Netherlands (N = 16,732, Mage = 43.79 years, age range = 16-75 years) and Germany (N = 12,167, Mage = 63.05 years, age range = 41-84 years). We applied local structural equation modeling in latent measurement models to examine rank-order stability. We further used local structural equation modeling within second-order latent growth models to investigate the age moderation on mean-level change (slopes) for each loneliness facet separately. Measurement invariance tests supported the psychometric comparability of social and emotional loneliness items across age groups and time points. Overall, results indicated that the developmental trajectories of social and emotional loneliness are age-invariant, while rank-order stability is comparatively low to moderate across the life span. This pattern may reflect the susceptibility of loneliness facets to individual life events and situational influences, distinguishing them from broader personality traits with higher stability patterns.

articles.title: Unpicking the Entanglement: A Perceived Causal Network of Biopsychosocial Factors for Mental Health
articles.authors: Emma Katharine Bridger; John Maltby; Daniel Nettle; Eiko I Fried; Lars Klintwall
articles.abstract: Research on causal beliefs about mental health often overlooks the complex entanglement of biopsychosocial factors and the role of social causes in particular. To unpick perceptions of the interplay between these factors, we pre-registered a study in which a UK-representative lay sample rated the causal influence between 20 biopsychosocial causes (two biological, seven psychological, 11 social) linked to mental health and poverty. Psychological factors were more strongly perceived to be effects than causes, and were perceived to be causally influenced by both biological and social factors. Stronger between-category than within-category causes reveal a contrast between lay perceptions and network approaches to mental health symptoms, which typically emphasise connections between psychological factors independent of biological and social causes. Overall, we conclude that this sample appreciate the complex interplay between adverse biopsychosocial factors, but primarily view this as operating from biological and social factors to psychological outcomes.

articles.title: Moderating effects of identity state and social dominance orientation on the relationship of status-based stressor exposure with self-reported health
articles.authors: Ganga Bey
articles.abstract: Although clarity on the social environment’s role in health disparities is growing, knowledge gaps remain regarding factors driving heterogeneity in the effects of social stressors on life course health. Identity-related characteristics, often unmeasured, may contribute to this variability and offer novel targets for public health intervention. We assessed whether two identity-related paradigms—identity vitality, a novel modifiable characteristic capturing inclusive versus exclusive self-concept, perceived intrinsic worth, and compassionate orientation (per the Identity Vitality-Pathology (IVP) model), and social dominance orientation (SDO)—moderated the effect of financial strain on health. Using 2025 data from 2,008 non-Hispanic Black and White adults, multivariable ordinal logistic regressions with interactions showed that among men under high financial strain, greater identity vitality and higher SDO were associated with higher self-rated health, whereas effects were weak or absent in women. Findings support the IVP hypothesis that modifiable identity factors can buffer stress-related health effects, with notable gender differences.

articles.title: Mixed-method randomised controlled trial of art-based intervention on trait beauty appreciation and mental health of young adults
articles.authors: mackenzie D Trupp; Maartje Wijnands; Amy De Roubaix; Aleksandra Igdalova; Blanca Thea Maria Spee; Eftychia Stamkou; Matthew Pelowski; Corina Greven
articles.abstract: Mental health concerns among young adults are rising sharply, yet scalable, evidence-based prevention strategies remain scarce. Trait beauty appreciation is consistently associated with better well-being and mental health and can be taught, making it a promising target for intervention. However, robust evidence is lacking because prior studies have not controlled for general awareness training, participation confounding, and/or placebo effects. This pre-registered 6-week mixed-methods RCT (N = 102) tested a novel two-week art-based beauty appreciation intervention against a structurally equivalent observation training active control to isolate beauty-specific effects. Both programs were reported to be credible and enjoyable and were community-based. The intervention increased trait beauty appreciation relative to the control, with large effects (ηp² = .16), although gains returned to near-baseline at the four-week follow-up. No group differences emerged for psychological distress or mental well-being, and near-zero between-group effect sizes suggest the results do not support an added short-term mental-health benefit of beauty-focused training over the matched active control. However, both groups showed comparable temporary reductions in distress, with qualitative interview data identifying largely overlapping mechanisms related to emotion and attention regulation, enhanced awareness, changes in thinking, and increased motivation. Sensory processing sensitivity, a hypothesised moderator, did not moderate outcomes. These findings show that trait beauty appreciation can be temporarily shifted, but this shift did not contribute to mental health beyond an ingredient-rich, general, observation-based active control.

articles.title: Unravelling the fatigue induced by a prolonged typing task
articles.authors: Léa Vidal; Mathieu Gruet; Maxime Bergevin; Thomas Mangin; Benjamin Pageaux
articles.abstract:
Objective
The primary aim of this study was to characterise fatigue induced by prolonged typing and its effects on cognitive, motor, and psychomotor functions. A secondary objective was to investigate whether prolonged typing influences the intention to engage in physical activity.
Background
Typing is an integral part of daily life, at home and at work. Because prolonged engagement in physical or cognitive activities can induce fatigue—characterized by objective and/or subjective manifestations—it is reasonable to assume that typing may induce fatigue.
Method
Thirty healthy adults (27±5 y.o., 21 females) participated in this within-subjects crossover pre-post design study. Participants completed 90-min of typing or documentary watching. Mental fatigue, effort, motivation, boredom, and perceived workload were assessed during each condition. Cognitive performance, typing performance, maximal voluntary force, and physical activity intention were measured before and after each task. Participants then completed a psychomotor task. 
Results
Typing induced greater fatigue than documentary watching (p=.003, η2p=.172), without impairing physical or cognitive performance. In contrast, psychomotor performance was reduced after typing (p=.018, d=0.457) but not after documentary watching. Regardless of condition, physical activity intention decreased after the tasks (p<.001, η2p=.469).
Conclusion
Prolonged typing induces a state of fatigue that selectively impairs psychomotor performance. Both typing and documentary watching reduced physical activity intentions, suggesting that prolonged screen-based sedentary activities may negatively influence physical activity intentions.
Application
The results highlight the need to address prolonged screen-based sedentary activities in occupational settings. Regular active breaks may help limit fatigue-related psychomotor declines and reduction in physical activity intentions.

articles.title: Who eats bugs, and what does it say about them? Disgust, stereotyping, and the social construction of the insect consumer
articles.authors: Katarzyna Goncikowska; Klaudia Modlinska; Wojciech Pisula
articles.abstract: The promotion of edible insects as a sustainable source of protein has gained considerable attention. However, consumer resistance remains high in Western countries, driven by disgust, food neophobia, and cultural taboos. Quantitative research has addressed the individual-level barriers to insect consumption, whilst the social and cultural meanings of entomophagy and its practitioners are under-explored, particularly in Central European contexts. The present study explored how Polish adults perceive insect-eating as a dietary practice, how they socially categorise and stereotype insect-eaters, and what psychological and sociocultural mechanisms underlie resistance to entomophagy. Semi-structured face-to-face interviews were conducted with 19 Polish adults (15F, 4M; age range 19–62 years) in August 2025. Data were analysed using Reflexive Thematic Analysis from a constructivist epistemological position. The findings suggest that social and political factors shape dietary rules and have implications for how we design culturally sensitive ways to encourage people to adopt them, and that resistance to certain types of food operates as a form of social categorisation, in which disgust can be transferred from food to its consumer. This approach to analysis allowed for an exploration of how individual barriers, such as disgust, may be transformed and reinforced by social processes and political polarisation. The study suggests that resistance to insect-based food was not organised solely around knowledge. Participants often recognised nutritional or ecological arguments, but still rejected insects because they were associated with disgust, contamination, social deviance, political meanings, and threats to familiar food identity. The study shows that insect-based foods may be rejected not only because they are unfamiliar, but also as markers through which consumers are socially imagined and evaluated.

articles.title: Examining the Relations Between Self-Construal, Culture, and Cognitive Dissonance Using the Induced-Compliance Paradigm
articles.authors: Tim Figureau; Daniel Priolo; Willem W. A. Sleegers; Kenneth G DeMarree; Omid Ghasemi; Amélie Gourdon-Kanhukamwe; Maxime Mauduy; Asil Ali Özdoğru; Robert M Ross; Bjørn Sætrevik
articles.abstract: Cognitive dissonance, traditionally conceptualized as a fundamental psychological process involving discomfort from inconsistent cognitions, may vary across cultures. These variations could be attributed to self-construal, or how individuals define themselves in relation to others. While previous cross-cultural research has predominantly used the free-choice paradigm, significant concerns have been raised regarding its procedural validity and the robustness of its findings. To address this issue, this registered report presents secondary analyses of a large-scale international dataset. We investigated the moderating role of cultural variables on dissonance effects within the induced-compliance paradigm across 18 countries (N = 3,804). We hypothesised that dissonance effects would be stronger for participants with higher individual-level self-construal scores (H1), in more individualistic countries (H2), and in countries with higher country-level self-construal scores (H3). Our results did not support these hypotheses, suggesting that cultural moderation may not generalize to the induced-compliance paradigm. We discuss the necessity of reassessing the procedure's reliability and clarifying the theoretical role of choice.

articles.title: Ethical Challenges and Opportunities in Global Big Team Science: Lessons from the Psychological Science Accelerator
articles.authors: Arushi Srivastava; Biljana Gjoneska; Sergio Barbosa; Marta Kowal; Maria Montefinese; Maja Becker; Erin Michelle Buchanan
articles.abstract: Global collaborations in psychological science offer enormous potential to improve generalizability, inclusivity, and equity but they also raise profound ethical challenges. In this paper, we draw on our experience as members of the Psychological Science Accelerator’s Ethics Committee to highlight the ethical tensions that arise in big team, cross-cultural psychological research. We synthesize insights from the literature and our committee’s applied work to identify common ethical dilemmas, such as consent processes, authorship, remuneration, and data sharing in global collaborations. We offer practical recommendations and reflective tools for researchers and institutions seeking to center ethical considerations in culturally diverse teams. Our goal is to help reframe global collaboration not only as a scientific opportunity but also as an ethical commitment to inclusivity, justice, and shared leadership.

articles.title: Systematic review and meta-analysis of psychosis-like visual perceptual disturbances and visual distortions in real and virtual environments
articles.authors: Chaya Antonia Chromik; Fabian David Mueller; William Chung Palmer; Kristina Sophie Hedelt; Marco Heide; Frederike Schröpfer; Sofia Eickhoff; Alexandra Korda; Bartosz Zurowski; Steven Silverstein
articles.abstract: Visual perceptual disturbances (VPDs) encompass an array of anomalous visual experiences occurring in the presence of an external stimulus. Within this hierarchy, visual distortions (VDs)—manifesting as transient low-level alterations—are frequently reported across the psychosis spectrum. Despite VPDs’ prevalence and hypothesized role in hallucination and delusion formation, they remain poorly characterized. Free-viewing eye-tracking (ET) and immersive virtual reality (VR) may offer ecologically valid approaches to capturing and simulating these experiences; however, their application to VPD research has not been systematically evaluated. This preregistered systematic review (PROSPERO: CRD42023494593), conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines, searched PubMed, Cochrane Library, PsycArticles, PsycInfo, and Scopus for studies using free-viewing ET or immersive technology in participants aged 15–65 years across the psychosis spectrum (CHR, FEP, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder). Two independent reviewers screened 3,135 records; 38 studies with 3,846 participants met inclusion criteria. These split into 31 ET and 7 immersive studies. Reporting quality was assessed using STROBE criteria. Results reveal that most ET studies focused on medicated individuals with established psychosis. They show reductions in saccade amplitude and spatial search metrics—patterns consistent with a stable trait marker that partly correlates with positive symptom severity. Preliminary evidence suggests possible predictive relevance in CHR populations due to persistent aberrations. Immersive studies used VR exclusively, primarily with non-clinical samples, and rarely investigated VPDs or VDs directly. Both technologies remain scarcely used in research specifically targeting VPDs. Combining free-viewing ET with VR may offer a promising approach for ecologically valid assessment of VPDs and for advancing early detection across the psychosis continuum.

articles.title: A Transparency Checklist for Theory-Driven Work in Psychology
articles.authors: Philipp Musfeld; Nele Freyer; Jens Lange; Felix D. Schönbrodt; Noah N'Djaye Nikolai van Dongen; Aaron Peikert; Anna-Lena Schubert; Rahel Geppert; Matthias Borgstede; Daniel Leising
articles.abstract: In light of the replication crisis and the Open Science Movement, transparency in psychological research has been increasingly recognized as being of key importance for cumulative knowledge gain. Numerous standards and checklists have been developed to improve transparency in reporting (see e.g., Aczel et al., 2019; Schubert et al., 2025). However, most of the available resources focus on methodological and statistical aspects of reporting, leaving the quality of theory development and theory testing largely unaddressed. To help close this gap, we develop a transparency checklist for reporting theoretical and theory-driven work in psychology, covering five main areas: 1) reporting of verbal theories; 2) reporting of formal models; 3) reporting theory evaluations by simulations; 4) reporting theory evaluations by empirical data; 5) reporting theory evaluations by comparison. The checklist is being actively developed in an iterative consensus process, in which sets of quality criteria are proposed, tested, evaluated by a large sample of experts, and then revised. Here, we present version 4 of the checklist, which is based on the input from 92 experts in the field. The checklist may be used to facilitate more precise and transparent reporting of theoretical work and can incentivize the role of theory in psychology when used in review and research assessment processes.

articles.title: Navigating the unknown: The sociocultural and metacognitive architecture of wisdom
articles.authors: Igor Grossmann; Nic M. Weststrate
articles.abstract: Psychological wisdom research has shifted from characterizing rare exemplars and desired outcomes to specifying processes that support sound judgment under uncertainty. Yet it has advanced along two siloed tracks: research on folk theories—cultural tools such as exemplars,  narratives, heuristics (including proverbs and maxims), and standards of judgment—and on scientific models of mechanisms involved in wise judgment. This review bridges these research tracks using a situated metacognitive lens: folk theories provide candidate attributes or strategies for action, whereas perspectival metacognition—the capacity to recognize epistemic limits, coordinate viewpoints, and track uncertainty and change—regulates their context-sensitive selection and use. We synthesize evidence connecting wisdom-related processes to emotional balance, relational well-being, cooperation, and reduced polarization, while noting boundary conditions. We show how this synthesis sharpens measurement trade-offs, highlighting limits of global self-report and advances in situated assessment. Finally, we summarize work on wisdom development and cultivation, and consider socio-ecological implications in an AI-shaped world.

articles.title: Altercentric biases in preschool children are associated with the supramarginal gyrus and motor cortex but not the mentalizing network
articles.authors: Chen Yang; Marie Luise Speiger; Philipp Berger; Charlotte Grosse Wiesmann
articles.abstract: As social beings, humans are involuntarily influenced by other people's perspectives. Such altercentric influences have been observed in both adults and young children. Recent evidence suggests that altercentric biases are behaviorally associated with explicit belief reasoning. This raises the question of whether altercentric biases rely on the same mentalizing processes as explicit belief reasoning, or instead reflect distinct processes that are nevertheless behaviorally linked. To address this question, we investigated cortical brain structure in preschool children (N = 37; age range = 3 - 5 years) in relation to their performance in an altercentric search task. We found that altercentric biases in children’s search for an object were associated with cortical structure in the right supramarginal gyrus, a region previously implicated in non-verbal action prediction, as well as in the precentral gyrus and supplementary motor regions. The association with motor regions suggests that participants' own sensorimotor representations may contribute to representing where another agent would search, potentially biasing their own actions. Together, these findings indicate that altercentric biases in the preschool years are linked to neural substrates outside the explicit mentalizing network, suggesting that they rely on distinct processes involving own sensorimotor representations.

articles.title: Simultaneous detection of patterned and random responses using unsupervised methods
articles.authors: Wenshuo Li; Carl F. Falk; Michael John Ilagan
articles.abstract:
Careless responding poses a growing threat to data quality and research validity in organizational and psychological sciences. Conventional detection methods often fail when data quality deteriorates or when multiple types of careless respondents coexist. To address these challenges, we propose and study several new methods that require neither measurement models nor labeled data, yet have the potential to detect random and patterned respondents simultaneously. In two simulation studies, we compare these methods to each other and to recently developed alternatives that are designed to detect only random or patterned responses. Empirical examples are also provided to shed additional light on
algorithm performance and possible boundary conditions. Methods that performed well in simultaneous detection were those that combine generation of a null distribution for each respondent (either by permutation or use of an n-gram model) with certain combinations of
outlier statistics for detecting random or patterned responses. However, such approaches were affected by sample size, the number of items, and length of patterns.
