Through this work, we provide new insights into detecting multiple types of carelessness and provide practical recommendations for improving data quality.

articles.title: Much of Politicians' Perceptual Error is Domain-General Processing of Proportions
articles.authors: Jack Lucas; Lior Sheffer; Christian Breunig
articles.abstract: Considerable research has documented large errors in politicians' perceptions of their constituents' characteristics and preferences. Despite many attempts, explanations of these pervasive errors using domain-specific factors, such as politicians' role perceptions, time in office, or institutional positions, have invariably failed. Here, we suggest that politicians' perceptual errors reflect a domain-general tendency to "hedge" perceptions toward a moderate prior, resulting in overestimation of small proportions and underestimation of large proportions. To measure this domain-general phenomenon, we combine all available recent studies of elite perceptual accuracy with novel surveys of sitting elected politicians in Canada, for a total of more than 70,000 perceptions from 12,860 politicians in seven countries. We find strong evidence that "midpoint hedging" explains a substantial fraction of the variation in politicians' perceptual errors. This tendency persists across studies, estimation tasks, countries, and issue domains. Our findings suggest that politicians are more likely to misperceive public opinion when it is most lopsided, meaning that policy responsiveness would tend to be undersupplied precisely when citizens demand it most.

articles.title: Learning about ‘Inflation’: Mapping Caregivers’ Mentalizing and Pedagogical Strategies that Support 8-9-year-old Children’s Learning of Abstract Concepts
articles.authors: Dora Szegedi; Gal Rozic; Peter Fonagy; Antonia Hamilton; Gabriella Vigliocco
articles.abstract: Understanding how children learn abstract concepts is critical for developmental theory and pedagogy, yet how caregivers support this process remains poorly understood. We examined semi-naturalistic interactions in 30 caregiver–child dyads (English-speaking children aged 8–9 years, 70% girls and 30% boys), in which caregivers (66% female, 34% male) explained 10 abstract financial concepts (e.g., inflation). Data was collected in London, UK between 2024 and 2025. Caregivers’ speech was annotated using a novel qualitative codebook. Generalized mixed- effects models indicated that three strategies were associated with higher odds of immediate and delayed learning: checking common ground (“Have you heard about inflation?”), using child- centered second-person hypotheticals (“Imagine you go to the store, and milk is now £2 instead of £1.”), and implicit comprehension checks (“When is there inflation?”). In contrast, personal examples (“Remember dad’s favorite chocolate was 75p...now it’s £1.35!”) were associated with lower odds of learning, suggesting a hindering effect of loading the child with salient but concept-irrelevant information. These findings highlight the role of perspective-taking and attention to children’s knowledge state in supporting abstract concept learning. Limitations include the use of English and a highly educated sample.

articles.title: Including social network quality in public health research: a scoping review on current practices
articles.authors: Rik Crutzen; Senne Maria Catharina Elisabeth Wijnen; Karoline Huth; Florian van Daalen; Nicole Dukers-Muijrers; Lisanne Catharina Johanna Steijvers
articles.abstract:
Aim: To (1) provide insight into how social network quality can be included in public health research and (2) generate directions for future research concerning the conceptualization and measurement of social network quality. 

Methods: A pre-registered scoping review using PubMed and APA PsychInfo as information sources. Sources of evidence were deemed eligible if they were full-text articles and reported on conceptualization and/or measurement of social network quality in public health research concerning humans. 

Results: In total, 78 articles were included. For most articles, the possible conceptualization used (if any) could only be implied based on the aspects that are measured in the reported studies. There is a large heterogeneity in aspects, with some recurring frequently (e.g., support, specific relationships, and satisfaction). This is indicative of differences in researchers’ conceptualization of social network quality.

Conclusion: The point of departure for future research is to advance the conceptualization of social network quality. The diversity in current conceptualizations and measurements of social network quality in public health research can even be beneficial. However, to benefit from this epistemic diversity, researchers need to make their conceptualizations and measurements explicit – and share them with others.

articles.title: Massaging Significance in the Age of Open Science: A Satirical Tutorial
articles.authors: Robert Böhm; Juergen Huber; Michael Kirchler
articles.abstract: The open science movement has made life considerably harder for researchers committed to the traditional craft of producing tidy, statistically significant stories from inconveniently untidy data that the publishing system still finds deeply attractive. Preregistration, data sharing, Registered Reports, and growing expectations of reproducibility have introduced unwelcome friction into once-fluid research workflows. In this satirical tutorial, we offer a service to the creatively empirical by documenting how significance may still be massaged in an era increasingly hostile to methodological improvisation. We identify three broad strategies. First, preregistration can be used less as a constraint than as a menu, allowing vagueness, selective reporting, and preregistration forks to preserve analytic flexibility. Second, exploratory findings can be dressed in confirmatory clothing by leaving the boundary between the two unmarked. Third, open science can be practiced opaquely, such that materials, data, and codes are technically available while remaining functionally unreachable. We then describe emerging threats to these strategies, including stricter preregistration standards, reproducibility checks, and mandatory study registration. Reassuringly for the creatively empirical, most of these reforms remain unlikely, as they would require meaningful changes to publishing incentives and coordinated effort among scientists, universities, and journals that academia rarely produces except in mission statements.

articles.title: Do Researchers Claim Their Findings Are Applicable to the Real World? An Empirical Study of Social and Personality Psychology Articles
articles.authors: Beth Clarke; Abdullah Sajjad; Nicholas Moodie; Simine Vazire
articles.abstract: The topics and questions that social and personality psychologists study attract a great deal of public interest. The level of evidence required to make an application claim should be high, in our opinion, to avoid misapplying evidence that could be harmful or ineffective, which could erode the public’s trust in the field. Our field’s challenges with reproducibility, common threats to construct, internal, external, and statistical conclusion validity, and the dearth of independent replications in the field, suggest that application claims should be rare. To investigate how often social and personality psychologists make application claims in their published articles, we extracted and categorized application claims from 669 original empirical social and personality psychology articles published from 2010 to 2020 across sevens. Just over one quarter of articles contained at least one application claim, and 1 in 20 articles contained an application claim in the Abstract. We did not find a significant association between having an application claim, and articles’ i) citation count or ii) Altmetric score, however, we did not have sufficient evidence to conclude that there is no association. The extent to which published application claims are warranted, and are actually applied, are open question for future research.

articles.title: The Morality-Effectiveness Disconnect of Pro-environmental Behaviors
articles.authors: Jareef Martuza; Hege Landsvik
articles.abstract: Not all pro-environmental behaviors are created equal—some are vastly more effective at mitigating climate change than others. Do people judge the more effective behaviors as proportionally more moral? Five main and six supplemental studies (N = 9,247) across five countries reveal a robust morality-effectiveness disconnect: pro-environmental behaviors that rank better on objective climate-mitigation potential rank worse on individual moral judgments. Laypeople fail to predict this pattern, believing that a positive relationship exists. Further, labeling behaviors by climate impact category reduces the morality-effectiveness disconnect, and detailed impact information reverses the disconnect and increases policy endorsement for more effective behaviors over less effective behaviors. Our results generally suggest that the disconnect stems from effectiveness misperceptions that can be corrected with accurate information, and personal support that favors less effective behaviors. Thus, people's moral intuition for climate action appears miscalibrated: moral cognition can undermine the collective welfare such cognition should presumably promote.

articles.title: Patterns of Everyday Physical Activity Captured with Smartphones Predict Satisfaction with Life
articles.authors: Maximilian Bergmann; Sarah Marie Müller; Ramona Schoedel; Gabriella M. Harari; Mirjam Stieger; Lara Kroencke; Samuel D. Gosling; Mathias Allemand; Mitja D Back; Markus Bühner
articles.abstract: Satisfaction with life (SWL) is central for mental well-being, making it a key focus for practitioners and researchers alike. Individual differences in SWL relate to self-reported patterns of everyday physical activity during daytime and overnight. However, these associations rarely replicate in objective physical activity measures, leaving real-world SWL manifestation unclear. Here, we use an interpretable machine learning approach to examine how physical activity patterns (across four weeks) captured via smartphones predict SWL in a diverse dataset (N = 2,272) spanning three countries, years, seasons, and sample demographics. The cross-validated results demonstrate that extremes (minima, maxima) and variation (standard deviation, skewness) in physical activity were particularly informative in robust out-of-sample predictions of SWL (r_Md = .18, r_IQR  = [.14, .21]), and that personality traits were associated with the extent of activity predictiveness. Overall, these findings highlight the importance of more nuanced approaches to studying associations between SWL and everyday physical activity.

articles.title: Learning words in diverse narrative contexts disrupts subsequent recall of their meanings
articles.authors: Rachael Catherine Hulme; Kate Nation; Jennifer M Rodd
articles.abstract: Recent research suggests that encountering words in varied narrative contexts may disrupt the earliest stage of learning (Hulme et al., 2023). The current experiments explore (i) how this diversity disadvantage changes over learning, and (ii) whether diversity primarily impacts encoding or subsequent recall. Adults (Experiment 1: N = 105; Experiment 2: N = 111) encountered novel words with invented meanings within narrative paragraphs across three days. Words were presented within one coherent narrative (low diversity) or across nine different narratives (high diversity). We replicated Hulme et al.'s (2023) results for word-form learning, finding moderate/strong evidence for null effects of diversity on word-form recall and recognition (Experiment 1), and speeded word-form recognition accuracy (Experiment 2), and only anecdotal evidence of a diversity benefit on response times. For word-meaning learning, Experiment 1 replicated the diversity disadvantage on word-meaning recall reported by Hulme et al. (2023; extreme evidence), but in Experiment 2 there was moderate evidence for a null effect on semantic feature recognition. Findings indicate that while narrative diversity does not strongly influence participants’ ability to encode or retain semantic features for new words, words learned in low-diversity conditions initially build more coherent contextually-bound representations that facilitate recall of semantic features.

articles.title: Embodied empathy towards patients with stroke
articles.authors: Rui Watanabe; Birgitta Paranko; Lauri Nummenmaa
articles.abstract:
Empathy toward patients with stroke may facilitate helping. Because stroke patients’ physical disabilities impact multiple body parts causing context-dependent difficulties during everyday activities, observers need to continuously monitor these difficulties to update their empathic responses. Here we tested whether observers embody the stroke patients’ motor difficulties during everyday locomotory task, and whether time-variable empathetic responses are coupled with helping motivation, 

Participants viewed videos of patients with stroke or healthy individuals walking and stepping over obstacles. In study 1, we measured participants’ bodily sensations, emotions felt while viewing the videos, and emotions attributed to the patient in the video. In study 2, participants provided continuously rated empathy and willingness to help while viewing the same videos. Participants attributed strong limb sensations to the patients’ stroke-affected body parts, and mirrored these sensations also in their own bodies. They also experienced and attributed stronger negative emotional experiences to the patients than healthy controls. Time-varying ratings revealed that empathy and willingness to help toward patients were engaged even before the difficulties in locomotion were evident. Empathy and willingness to help ratings also increased continuously over time and were strongly coupled.

These findings suggest that empathy toward patients with stroke is embodied, through sensations specific to the stroke-affected limbs and bodily regions. Empathy and helping motivation are rapidly engaged, dynamically updated as situations unfold, and tightly coupled over time. Overall these results suggest an embodied basis of empathy toward patients with stroke and its close relationship with helping motivation.

articles.title: Model Checking for Vector Autoregressive Models
articles.authors: Jonas M B Haslbeck; Joran Jongerling; Björn S. Siepe; Sacha Epskamp; Lourens Waldorp
articles.abstract: Time series have become pervasive in psychological science, and Vector Autoregressive (VAR) models are now among the most popular tools for studying within-person dynamics in such data. However, researchers rarely check systematically how well a VAR model fits their data. This is problematic because model misfit can lead both to incorrect interpretations of model parameters and to missed structure in the data that would be theoretically interesting. We provide a tutorial that explains the theory behind model checking, discusses the most common types of VAR model misspecification in psychological time series, and introduces diagnostics for detecting them using plots and simulations. In addition, we provide code to extract predictions and residuals from popular software packages, along with the new R-package VARcheck, which allows researchers to create extensive diagnostic plots with only a few lines of code. We then apply these tools to assess the fit of a multilevel VAR model estimated on a typical empirical dataset of emotion measurements from 179 people over three weeks. We conclude by discussing three complementary ways in which model checking can advance psychological science using time series.

articles.title: Cognitive predictors of reading in Spanish: a large-scale study spanning primary and secondary education
articles.authors: Amaia Carrion-Castillo; Marie Lallier; Manuel Carreiras
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Reading in transparent orthographies relies on phonological phonological processes, rapid automatized naming (RAN), and orthographic processing, yet their relative contributions across development remain unclear. We investigated (i) how these abilities jointly predict reading efficiency across grades, (ii) the developmental stability of these effects, and (iii) whether diagnosed dyslexia reflects a categorically distinct profile.

Method: We analyzed data from a cross-sectional sample of 2,971 Spanish children (ages 6-16) with phonological, rapid naming, and orthographic tasks. Principal component analysis yielded six cognitive components. Mixed-effects models tested each component as a predictor of reading efficiency, with school-grade as moderator. Sensitivity analyses examined word and nonword accuracy and speed measures separately. We compared 44 dyslexia-diagnosed children and the low-reading efficiency tail, to typically developing readers on reading and cognitive components.

Results: All six components independently predicted reading efficiency (all p < .001), with RAN explaining the most variance (16%), followed by the phonological processing component (6.7%), and the rest explaining between 5.1% and 0.9 of additional variance. Only the phonological processing component showed school-grade moderation, with stronger effects in earlier grades. RAN dominated for speed-based reading outcomes, while phonological processing component was strongest for accuracy. Both low-reading groups showed deficits in RAN, phonological processing, and orthographic letter identity relative to norms, with statistically indistinguishable cognitive profiles.

Conclusion: Reading in Spanish is supported by RAN, phonological and orthographic processes throughout development. The similar cognitive profiles of diagnosed and undiagnosed poor readers support a dimensional view of reading difficulties.

articles.title: Actionable cognitive maps for spatial and conceptual thinking
articles.authors: Roberto Bottini; Simone Viganò; Yangwen Xu; Jean-Philippe van Dijck; Elger Abrahamse; Giovanni Pezzulo
articles.abstract:
Cognitive maps provide a powerful way to compress experience into low-dimensional
structure that supports flexible learning, inference, and generalization across spatial and
conceptual domains. Yet relational structure must ultimately be converted into action and
decision-making. Here we advance a unifying account of how hippocampal–entorhinal
cognitive maps become action-relevant, translating core principles from spatial navigation to
nonspatial declarative knowledge. We first synthesize evidence that conceptual navigation
engages complementary allocentric and egocentric reference frames. We then operationalize
egocentric coding in conceptual spaces as an attentional “point of view,” and show how mental
actions can be implemented as shifts of attention over relational structures. Finally, we outline
how reference-frame transformations and state-updating mechanisms—well characterized in
spatial navigation—can generalize to conceptual spaces, yielding representations that support
both overt behavior and internal mental operations.

articles.title: Delay Means Death: Measuring Public Support or Rejection of Climate Delay Discourses
articles.authors: Adrian Dominik Wojcik; Michał Główczewski; Taciano L Milfont
articles.abstract: Addressing the climate crisis requires a nuanced understanding of the beliefs that undermine individual and collective action. While climate denial has been extensively studied, less attention has been paid to "climate delayism"—a set of beliefs that, while not necessarily negating anthropogenic climate change, serve to justify inaction. These discourses of delay typically involve shifting responsibility, promoting non-transformational solutions, emphasizing the flaws of climate policies, and downplaying climate impacts. Although delayism has been identified as a rhetorical strategy among corporate and political elites, its prevalence within the general public remains under-researched. This study fills that gap by examining the prevalence of climate delayism beliefs and their associations with climate anxiety and denial based on two representative samples of citizens in Poland and the UK (N=1,038). Our results show that: (1) the overall factor structure of climate delayism beliefs is similar in both countries; (2) greater adherence to climate delayism is associated with lower negative climate emotions and higher climate denial; and (3) critically, some delayist beliefs are held even by respondents who believe in climate change and are concerned about it. We discuss these findings and propose that as outright denialist beliefs wane, delay discourses are becoming a more predominant and insidious obstacle to transformative climate action.

articles.title: How does the developing mind construct a self?
articles.authors: Victoria Southgate
articles.abstract:
Our capacity to think about ourselves is a developmental achievement that, when it
emerges, profoundly changes cognition. Currently, there are no mechanistic accounts of
where a concept of self could come from and empirical investigation within
contemporary developmental science is surprisingly absent. Drawing on a wealth of
data from cognitive neuroscience and developmental psychology, I propose a new
mechanistic account of the ontogeny of self-awareness anchored in infants’
interoceptive signals. However, this is a social cognitive account in which infants’
expectations about others’ attention are deeply entwined with the modulating effect
that others’ attention has on infants’ awareness of their interoceptive state and which
together provide the impetus for infants to generate a conceptual representation of self
de novo.

articles.title: Daydreaming in the Age of Self-Identified ADHD: Revisiting the Short Imaginal Processes Inventory in US and Hong Kong Adolescents
articles.authors: Hui Yun LI; Ho Nam Cheung; Mojtaba Habibi Asgarabad
articles.abstract: Emerging social media environments have contributed to a growing group of adolescents who self-identify with ADHD, reframing ADHD from a strictly clinical category into a socially meaningful identity. This identity may shape how adolescents interpret mind wandering and respond to daydreaming measures. Because such meanings are embedded in cultural narratives, adolescents may show culture-specific endorsement patterns. Therefore, validating the factor structure of daydreaming among this population and establishing measurement invariance across cultures are important for supporting the cross-cultural validity of the Short Imaginal Processes Inventory (SIPI; Huba et al., 1981). We tested the SIPI’s three-factor structure and measure invariance in 666 adolescents from the United States (n = 452, Mage = 15.50, SD = 1.33) and Hong Kong (n = 214, Mage = 15.70, SD = 1.16). Subscales showed good to excellent internal consistency (α = .76–.94). Confirmatory factor and measurement invariance analyses supported the SIPI’s three-factor structure and cross-cultural comparability among self-identified ADHD adolescents.

articles.title: Modifying Empathic Disequilibrium Through a Brief Mindfulness-Based Meditation Training
articles.authors: Ido Shalev; Emily Hayun; Alal Eran; Florina Uzefovsky
articles.abstract: Whether a person feels overwhelmed by others' emotions or remains detached from them may depend not on how much empathy they have, but on the balance between the cognitive and emotional components of empathy. An imbalance between emotional and cognitive empathy, termed empathic disequilibrium, has consistently distinguished clinical from non-clinical groups across a range of conditions, independent of overall empathy levels. Given these links, empathic disequilibrium may be experienced by some as distressing, yet whether it is malleable and what mechanisms may underlie such change remains untested. Building on the view that empathic disequilibrium acts as a form of emotion dysregulation in interpersonal contexts, and on its links to emotional reactivity, this study tested whether a brief mindfulness intervention can modify empathic disequilibrium. A total of 157 psychology students were assigned to ten days of daily, 10 minute guided mindfulness training (n = 89) or to a control group involving emotion regulation lessons and daily mood ratings (n = 68). Empathic disequilibrium and emotional reactivity were assessed at baseline and post-intervention using validated self-report measures. Compared with the control group, mindfulness training reduced both forms of empathic disequilibrium. Contrary to our hypothesis, this reduction was not mediated by changes in emotional reactivity, even though reactivity itself decreased. Complementary Bayesian and propensity-score sensitivity analyses supported the robustness of these effects. These findings provide the first evidence that empathic disequilibrium is modifiable in the general population, and suggest that mechanisms beyond emotional reactivity sustain this interpersonal imbalance.

articles.title: Self-Rated Health as a Prospective Predictor of Daily Symptoms and Illness Behavior
articles.authors: Nofar Mizrachi; Liron Rozenkrantz; PBL lab
articles.abstract: Objective: Self-rated health (SRH), the simple question “How would generally you rate your health?”, is one of the strongest known predictors of morbidity and mortality. Yet, it remains unclear whether this single subjective evaluation also captures more common, everyday physical health experiences. Understanding whether SRH predicts everyday physical health would enable a more precise analysis of the mediators and moderators of this relationship, thereby extending our understanding of how subjective health evaluations unfold in everyday life. The present preregistered study examined whether SRH prospectively predicts short-term physical symptoms and illness behavior in everyday life in the general population. Methods: Participants completed repeated online surveys over eight weeks, reporting their SRH, physical symptoms, illness behavior, and psychological characteristics. Results: Cross-sectional analyses showed that SRH was strongly associated with physical symptoms, beyond demographics, diagnosed health conditions, and socio-economic factors. Longitudinal cross-lagged models revealed that SRH prospectively predicted both physical symptoms and illness behavior at later follow-ups, whereas neither symptoms nor illness behavior predicted SRH at any assessment. Exploratory analyses further revealed that these effects were stronger among individuals with higher health anxiety and lower mental resilience. Conclusions: Together, these findings demonstrate that SRH captures not only long-term health risks but also short-term fluctuations in everyday physical health and illness behavior. Therefore, SRH may offer a simple tool for identifying individuals at risk for heightened physical symptoms experiences and may serve as a potential target for interventions aimed at improving everyday health.

articles.title: Beyond the Present Moment: Why Mindfulness Research Needs a Historical Perspective
articles.authors: Johannes Alfons Karl
articles.abstract: Background: Mindfulness research has drawn extensively on the construct's long textual record for conceptual definitions but rarely treated that record as a source of empirical evidence. As a result, contemporary work captures cross-sectional variation across present-day populations without addressing the longer historical trajectories that produced it, leaving open questions about how cultural and ecological forces have shaped mindfulness over centuries and millennia. Objective: This paper develops the case for a historical psychology of mindfulness, positioned between evolutionary work on phylogenetic timescales and cross-cultural work on contemporary populations. Methods: It reviews methodological approaches that make diachronic analysis tractable, including top-down dictionary construction, bottom-up emic curation, distributed and contextualized construct representation, large language model prompting, and generative simulation with historical large language models, and surveys source materials across canonical and commentarial traditions, practical and instructional literature, first-person accounts, general published text, and non-textual cultural artefacts. Results: Drawing on cross-cultural psychology, the paper outlines the construct, method, and item biases that the temporal dimension compounds. Conclusion: It closes with three questions for a research agenda: how mindfulness is distributed across populations, what shapes it, and whether contemplative traditions converged on a shared phenomenon.

articles.title: Artificial intelligence as work design: A scoping review of motivational and demotivational mechanisms in professional work contexts
articles.authors: Samira Hamouche; Josette M. P. Gevers; Jan C. Fransoo; Toni Waefler
articles.abstract:
Work design has long been recognized as a central determinant of intrinsic motivation, well-being, and performance. As artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly enters professional work, it should not be understood merely as a technical tool, but as a force that directly reshapes working conditions by altering how tasks are structured, decisions are made, feedback is delivered, and workers interact with their work and with one another. This makes work design and motivational theories especially important for the design and implementation of AI systems. To clarify this fragmented field within human-AI interaction research, including closely related work on human-AI collaboration and human-AI teaming, we conducted a scoping review of AI in professional, knowledge-intensive work contexts, focusing on how different AI configurations relate to motivation and demotivation. Following PRISMA-ScR, we reviewed 31 empirical and non-empirical sources published between 2009 and 2025.
Only three sources proposed explicit motivational AI design frameworks, suggesting that motivation is still more often treated as an outcome of AI use than as a direct design objective. Across the corpus, AI was described as motivationally supportive when it preserved meaningful human influence, provided developmental feedback, or reduced routine burden without hollowing out meaningful work. Demotivational risks were described when AI expanded surveillance, narrowed self-organization, prescribed work too tightly, or translated social relations into comparison and performance signals. These risks were especially prominent in conceptual discussions of managerial or algorithmic-management configurations, whereas empirical evidence was more concentrated in assistive AI settings.

articles.title: Evaluating Browser-Based Administration of a Repeated Self-Control Choice Task on Participants’ Own Smartphones: Pragmatic and Controlled Comparisons
articles.authors: Aya Katayama; Tomohisa Asai; Daisuke Saeki
articles.abstract: Browser-based experiments administered on participants’ own smartphones offer practical advantages for psychological research, but their comparability with laboratory administration remains task- and context-dependent. This study examined whether a browser-based implementation of a repeated self-control choice task (SCT) administered on participants’ own smartphones yields data comparable to those obtained under laboratory administration. Rather than treating comparability as a single criterion, we evaluated mean levels, changes across blocks, preservation of participants’ relative standing, mean bias, scale differences, and procedural stability. In Experiment 1, we conducted a pragmatic comparison in undergraduate and non-student adult samples, allowing multiple implementation-related factors to vary as they would in realistic web-based research contexts. Self-control choices increased across blocks in all groups. Mean levels were broadly similar between web-based and laboratory administration among undergraduates, whereas adults showed fewer self-control choices in the web condition; however, this difference reflected a pragmatic bundle of differences in recruitment, testing context, device, software, and supervision, rather than an isolated administration-mode effect. Experiment 2 used a within-participant, same-device design in which participants completed the SCT under both web and lab conditions. In the with-loss condition, mean-level web–lab differences were small, but participant-level correspondence was accompanied by substantial uncertainty and possible scale differences. Comparability varied across task conditions, with weaker correspondence in conditions less suited to discriminating individual differences. An auxiliary N-back task showed no clear evidence of general performance disruption under web-based administration. Overall, browser-based SCT administration on participants’ own smartphones was practically feasible, but mean-level similarity alone was insufficient to establish methodological comparability. Evaluations of web-based repeated-choice tasks should distinguish average performance from preservation of individual differences, systematic bias, scale differences, and implementation robustness.

articles.title: ANDrate toolbox: A Tutorial for collecting behavioral data in naturalistic stimuli in a python based toolbox
articles.authors: Eric Rui Wen Wang; Rui Jing; Haiyan Wu
articles.abstract: Naturalistic stimuli, such as movies, can provide ecological validity and examine human perception and cognition in real-world scenarios. As a result, it is drawing more and more attention from the psychological and cognitive neuroscience community. However, one drawback is that these types of studies usually have no explicit behaviour measures and require raters to provide group-level consensus annotations for the stimuli, such as consensus boundaries and emotional ratings. Here, we introduce ANDrate. ANDrate is an open-source annotation toolbox; it implements boundary segmentation, event annotation, and continuous two-dimensional measurement reporting. The toolbox also supports webcam recording capabilities to ensure data quality. Finally, ANDrate implements algorithms for calculating consensus boundaries and ratings, and enables visualization and the export of standard data formats. It is particularly well-suited for applications in naturalistic experiments, such as research providing continuous emotional states and event cognition for films.

articles.title: Cognitive Modeling of Autobiographical Memory Test: Theoretical Approach to the Mechanisms of Overgeneral Memory
articles.authors: Noboru Matsumoto; Michael David Lee; Keisuke Takano; Tom Joseph Barry; David John Hallford
articles.abstract: Overgeneral autobiographical memory is a well-known transdiagnostic feature of psychopathology, commonly assessed using the Autobiographical Memory Test (AMT), a cued recall method. AMT performance has been scored using heterogeneous outcome measures across studies, including differences in whether specific or general memories are treated as the primary outcome and in how omission responses are handled, raising concerns about replicability. In the present study, we propose a model–based scoring framework for the AMT, termed the accessibility model, which decomposes memory retrieval into three latent parameters: accessibility of specific memories (ρ), accessibility of general memories (γ), and the probability of initiating additional retrieval following goal-incongruent memory retrieval (φ). We firstly conducted simulation studies to examine model behavior and estimation stability, demonstrating that reliable parameter estimation requires approximately 15 trials (cue words) per condition. Next, we tested theoretically derived predictions using three empirical datasets. Results showed that concrete cues yielded higher ρ values, whereas abstract cues yielded higher γ values, consistent with theories of cue-driven accessibility. Instructional manipulations showed that traditional instructions emphasizing specific retrieval goals (AMT-TI), relative to minimal instructions that allowed reporting of any memory (AMT-MI), increased φ. Retrieval instructions also modulated memory accessibility, yielding higher ρ and lower γ in AMT-TI than AMT-MI. Furthermore, depressive symptom severity was associated with higher γ and lower φ for negative cues, indicating heightened accessibility of negative overgeneral memories and reduced likelihood of initiating further retrieval. We highlight several avenues for future refinement of the model and its broader theoretical implications.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the Preparedness to Disclose Non-Suicidal Self-Injury Scale
articles.authors: Kassandra Hon; Penelope Hasking; Stephen P Lewis; Mark Edward Boyes
articles.abstract: The disclosure of nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) can be a critical step in recovery by enabling access to social support, clinical intervention, and emotional validation. However, little is known about the psychological and contextual factors that shape individuals’ decisions to disclose NSSI. The present study developed and provided initial validation for the Preparedness to Disclose Nonsuicidal Self-Injury Scale (PDNS), a novel measure designed to assess individuals’ psychological readiness to disclose their experiences of NSSI. Seventy-five preliminary items were administered to 675 university students (77% female; M = 22.20 years, SD = 4.83), 64% of whom had previously disclosed their NSSI. Principal axis factoring revealed a three-factor structure (Self-Efficacy to Disclose, Interpersonal Motives, and Situational Readiness). The five highest loading items on each factor were retained, resulting in a 15-item scale that reliably differentiated individuals who had disclosed from those who had not. Evidence of measurement invariance across individuals who had or had not disclosed their NSSI supported the robustness and generalisability of the PDNS. Correlations with measures of theoretically related constructs provided support for the scale’s convergent and discriminant validity. These findings indicate that preparedness to disclose NSSI is a dynamic, multifaceted process shaped by social-cognitive factors. The PDNS provides a psychometrically sound tool for research and clinical practice, offering a framework to support safe and adaptive disclosure experiences among individuals who self-injure.

articles.title: AI Image Generation Exhibits East Asian Prototype Bias
articles.authors: Ishika Ray; Yuichi Shoda
articles.abstract: AI image generation systems are increasingly used to create visual content, including depictions of people. The present study investigated whether images of “Asian” individuals generated by such systems are representative of the diversity in Asian populations. Two text-to-image [IR1.1]AI models (ChatGPT’s Dall-E, and Gemini’s Imagen) were prompted to generate images of ‘an Asian person,’ yielding a total of eight images. One hundred and ninety-one U.S.-based raters were then asked to identify the likely country of origin for the individual depicted in each image. For any given image, an average of 66.9% raters (95% CI [54.1%, 79.5%]) selected an East Asian country as the most likely origin. This observed proportion is substantially higher than would be expected based on real-world distributions, exceeding both the approximately 38% of Asian Americans who identify as East Asian (e.g., Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Mongolian) and the approximately 34% of the global population residing in countries commonly considered to be in Asia. These findings suggest AI training data may reflect “Asian = East Asian” association, systematically underrepresenting South Asian and Southeast Asian populations. Such biases have important implications for representational equity in AI-generated content.

articles.title: Multidimensional Assessment of Smartphone and Social Media Use: Links with Sleep and Mental Health
articles.authors: Emma Caitlin Sullivan; Umar Toseeb; Lisa Henderson
articles.abstract: There is widespread concern that adolescent mental health is at risk in our “always connected” world. For example, smartphone and social media (S/SM) use around bedtime may displace sleep, putting mental health at risk. However, most research examining these relationships relies on undifferentiated measures of S/SM behaviours, with a need to move beyond oversimplistic models to advance mechanistic understanding. We surveyed 2,333 11-19-year-olds on an array of S/SM behaviours, using exploratory factor analysis to reveal three latent dimensions: (i) S/SM time (i.e., time spent on S/SM), (ii) problematic/negative S/SM use and (iii) SM platform use. Preregistered hypotheses tested whether these dimensions predicted self-reported sleep quality, and whether sleep quality would mediate any associations with depression and anxiety symptoms. Higher S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM use predicted poorer sleep quality and higher symptoms of anxiety and depression. In contrast, SM platform use was associated with lower anxiety symptoms. As well as direct links between S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM and depressive and anxiety symptoms, there were also indirect associations via sleep quality. The present study highlights the need for future longitudinal research to incorporate rich and multidimensional measures of S/SM alongside objective sleep assessments to robustly examine the role of sleep as a critical mediator in causal pathways to mental health.

articles.title: Spontaneous Perception of Visuospatial Ensembles
articles.authors: Gabriel Waterhouse; Sami Ryan Yousif
articles.abstract: Look out at a forest. What do you see? You see visual masses of green; you see large numbers of leaves; you see the various sizes of the many different trees. Is one of those features more salient than the others? According to a popular view in cognitive science, number is perceptually prioritized over other visuospatial ensemble properties like density and size, such that when you look out at a forest, what you see first and foremost is the number of trees (or trunks, or leaves) rather than their size or their density, etc. The problem with these arguments is that they mostly hinge on comparisons to only one other dimension: cumulative area. Here, we ask a very simple question: Might there be some other dimension that is prioritized over number? Across five unique paradigms and thirteen experiments with over 2,000 participants, we show that human adults prioritize average size over number, such that they are more likely to (a) categorize, (b) explicitly match, (c) notice changes in, and (d) identify extreme instances of that dimension. We additionally show that children behave the same way. For this reason, we argue against the universal salience of number, noting that these ‘competitive’ approaches (i.e., in which dimensions are compared against each other) are often context dependent.

articles.title: Does perceived chronic stress really impair decision-making capacity? Evidence from decisions made under varying computational demands
articles.authors: Karlo Doroc; Nitin Yadav; Juan Pablo Franco; Carsten Murawski
articles.abstract: Chronic stress is a modern health epidemic, yet whether its impairing effects on basic cognitive capacities generalise to higher-order decision-making remains underexplored. To address this, we conducted a large (N=355), preregistered online experiment measuring perceived chronic stress (PCS), five cognitive capacities, and decision-making capacity (via the knapsack optimisation task). This paradigm provides an objective and risk preference-free measure of decision-making capacity. Importantly, it also allows us to systematically modulate the computational demands of each decision using objective, task-independent features. Surprisingly, in a broadly representative US sample, higher PCS within a normal range was largely unrelated to decision-making capacity or cognitive performance, even on computationally demanding decisions. PCS could not credibly predict decision quality, decision time, click-based exploration of the search space, metacognitive judgements or the types of errors made. Our results challenge a generalised view of chronic stress' negative impact and we discuss potential explanations that may reconcile differential effects observed across the literature.

articles.title: The Disappearance of the Social?  A Conceptual Critique of the Para- and Post-Social Media Debate
articles.authors: Miira Hill
articles.abstract: Recent contributions by danah boyd (2026) and Törnberg & Rogers (2026) have argued that social media platforms have undergone a fundamental transformation: what once enabled reciprocal social interaction has become a landscape of parasocial observation, algorithmic broadcasting, and post-social fragmentation. This essay takes these empirical diagnoses seriously while subjecting their conceptual foundations to critical scrutiny. It argues that both contributions operate with an implicitly normative and analytically underdetermined concept of sociality, one that reduces a theoretically complex category to a historically particular manifestation. Drawing on the sociology of knowledge (Berger & Luckmann), communicative constructivism (Knoblauch, Keller), actor-network theory (Latour), feminist materialism (Haraway), and the concept of object-centred sociality (Knorr Cetina), the essay argues that sociality was never independent of its material and technical mediations, and that the relationship to objects, algorithms, and non-human actors is itself a form of sociality rather than its absence. A further objection is epistemological: ‘social media’ was never a scientifically precise category but an ethnocategory, an industry term and journalistic shorthand whose normative charge has been unreflectively adopted as an analytical framework. The essay concludes that the current debate diagnoses less the end of ‘social media’ than the crisis of a never-sufficiently-defined scientific category, and calls for a more systematic engagement with theoretical resources, available both within and beyond communication research, that may help to specify what the social is, and where it resides in contemporary digital environments.

articles.title: Out of sight, out of cart? The effect of a Nudge(+) that reduced visibility of meat promotions on supermarket shopping behavior
articles.authors: Lotte Patricia de Lint; Rachelle de Vries; Wilhelmina Elisabeth Adriana Metje van Rookhuijzen; Gerrit Gort; Emely de Vet
articles.abstract: Supermarkets have a major influence on food purchases and thus represent a good starting point for shifting consumption toward plant-based options. In a field study within a Dutch discount supermarket chain (10  intervention, 10 control stores, 11 weeks), we evaluated the effect of two interventions that were each implemented during half of the weeks on animal- and plant-based purchasing. The Nudge (N) intervention reduced fresh meat visibility by moving promotional products from end-cap displays to regular shelf locations with standard-sized price tags, and modestly increased plant-based visibility through recipes and footstep cues. In the Nudge+ (N+) intervention condition, communication materials containing a reflective cue aimed at activating plant-based goals were additionally placed at store entrances and the fresh meat aisle. Results showed that within the fresh meat and meat replacement section, the ratio of plant- to animal-based protein sold by weight, as well as plant-based sales, did not change significantly under either intervention. However, animal-based protein sales by weight significantly decreased within this section (N:-4.75%, N+:-6.37%), with no significant difference between interventions. Outside this section, neither intervention had conclusive effects on (non-targeted) animal-based purchases, though trends suggested animal-based purchasing increased. No changes in total store revenue were detected. Most shoppers did not notice the interventions yet were positive or neutral when informed. A nudge that includes reducing meat's promotional visibility appears to be an effective strategy for decreasing animal-based purchasing in a real-world supermarket while preserving commercial performance and customer acceptance.

articles.title: Political Budget Cycles in Autocracies: The Role of Religious Seasons and Political Collective Action
articles.authors: Ahmed Ezzeldin Mohamed
articles.abstract: This article examines whether Ramadan is associated with expansionary fiscal policy in Egypt between 2006 and 2019. It finds that Ramadan is associated with modest increases in government expenditure and welfare spending, but this relationship depends on the level of political threats facing the regime. Spending is higher in Ramadan when preceded by more anti-regime collective action.

articles.title: Accounting for local economic benefit in tourism accommodation: a lean methodology for property-level reporting
articles.authors: Glynn O’Leary; Harold Goodwin; Xavier Font; Barry Gray; Eleanor Muller; Tenele Motsa; Elna Petersen
articles.abstract: This article contributes to sustainable tourism impact measurement by developing and illustrating a lean local economic benefit accounting methodology for accommodation enterprises. Drawing on three cases, the article shows how routine financial accounts can be reclassified to make local wages, procurement, community payments, accommodation overflow and other locally retained flows visible. The method does not claim to measure full economic impact, net additionality or multiplier effects. Rather, it provides a transparent lower-bound account of enterprise-mediated local value that can support management decisions about procurement, supplier development and local embeddedness. The cases show that the accounting logic travels across remote, refurbishment and annual-reporting contexts, but also that further standardisation is needed before the method can support benchmarking across properties.

articles.title: DOEM-UNIBO: Un dataset su Otto e mezzo, un talk show politico italiano
articles.authors: Tommaso Aicardi; Chloe Papadopoulou; Marco Albertini
articles.abstract: L'articolo presenta DOEM, un nuovo dataset longitudinale per lo studio della comunicazione politica televisiva in Italia. Il dataset raccoglie le trascrizioni complete di 2.889 episodi del talk show politico \textit{Otto e mezzo}, trasmesso su La7 tra il 4 aprile 2011 e il 10 giugno 2025. Le trascrizioni sono state prodotte a partire dall'audio degli episodi tramite modelli di automatic speech recognition basati su Whisper-AI e successivamente sottoposte a procedure di pulizia, controllo e normalizzazione. Oltre al testo completo delle puntate, DOEM include metadati sulla data di trasmissione e sugli ospiti presenti. Il paper illustra la struttura del dataset e presenta alcuni esempi di utilizzo per l'analisi della copertura mediatica di eventi politici, dell'attenzione verso specifiche policy issues e dello stile comunicativo degli attori politici. DOEM costituisce una risorsa originale per studi di comunicazione politica, stile della leadership, agenda setting e, più in generale, promuove l'utilizzo di approcci computazionali e di  "text as data" negli studi della comunicaizone politica in Italia.

articles.title: Education, family complexity and heterogeneous causal effects of parental separation
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Satu Helske; Jani Erola; Jouni Helske
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study examines educational heterogeneity in the impact of parental separation on educational attainment, considering the full sequence of childhood family structural changes.
Background: Studies have consistently associated parental separation with lower socioeconomic achievement. However, the underlying causes remain unclear, possibly due to self-selection or continued instability in the family environment.
Method: This study employs structural causal modeling and Bayesian logistic regression to investigate the causal effect of childhood family structural changes on adult educational attainment using Finnish full-population register data (n = 272,962), focusing on cohorts born in 1987–1991 and their family members.
Results: A substantial portion of the 17-percentage-point disadvantage of children from separated families was due to the selection of families into separation and further structural changes. Separation effects followed a U-shaped pattern, with the largest negative effects among families in the middle of the educational gradient. Stepparents did not exert positive or negative effects, except for highly educated parents’ children benefiting from stable stepfathers. Recurring family instability had the largest negative impact.
Conclusion: The curvilinear relationship between parental and child education may partly explain the mixed results of previous studies.
Implications: Given that recurring family instability was shown to be the most detrimental to children’s educational attainment, low-threshold guidance and support to aspiring and existing blended families might have the largest impacts on children of separated parents.

articles.title: From Exposure to Belief: Neighborhood Female Employment and Normative Beliefs Regarding Maternal Employment
articles.authors: Kerstin Ostermann; Marie-Fleur Philipp; Eileen Peters
articles.abstract: Individuals’ beliefs regarding maternal employment are central to the reproduction of gender inequalities in paid and unpaid work. While prior research has linked gender beliefs to personal characteristics and national policies, the influence of local contexts remains underexplored. Drawing on the perspective of gender as a social structure framework and social norm theory, we conceptualize neighborhoods as socio-cultural reference frames where local norms reflect and reinforce normative gender beliefs. Combining the German Panel Study 'Labour Market and Social Security' (2011/2016) with georeferenced administrative data on 1x1km grid cells, we investigate how neighborhood-level female employment patterns shape normative beliefs about maternal full-time employment in Germany. Support for a later maternal return to full-time employment is greater in neighborhoods with higher shares of marginal female employment and where traditional employment forms dominate, even after controlling for the socio-economic composition of surrounding grid cells. In contrast, higher local shares and dominance of female full-time employment among women are associated with greater support for an earlier maternal return to full-time employment. These associations are most pronounced among parents, for whom gender norms around maternal employment are most salient. The findings highlight the role of the neighborhood level for the everyday reproduction of gender inequalities.

articles.title: Instrumental Support Before and After Retirement: Longitudinal Evidence  from Europe
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Hans Hämäläinen; Bruno Arpino; Antti O Tanskanen; Aïda Solé-Auró; Mirkka Danielsbacka
articles.abstract:
As populations age and the share of retirees increases across Europe, the question of whether 
individuals stay active after retirement is increasingly salient. Retirees are known to provide 
more instrumental support than their working counterparts. However, most previous studies 
have relied on between-group comparisons of retirees and employed individuals, leaving it 
unclear how the transition into retirement affects changes in instrumental support within 
individuals over time. Using eight waves of the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in 
Europe covering 19 countries between 2004–2022 (n=7,422 observations from 3,711 
individuals aged 50+), we employed fixed-effects regression and found that the transition 
from employment to retirement was associated with an increase in instrumental support 
provision outside the household. Moreover, the association was stronger in countries with 
higher total social expenditures. These findings suggest that retirement may facilitate 
engagement in helping others, with the magnitude of the association varying across 
institutional contexts.

articles.title: Education, family complexity and heterogeneous causal effects of parental separation
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Satu Helske; Jani Erola; Jouni Helske
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study examines educational heterogeneity in the impact of parental separation on educational attainment, considering the full sequence of childhood family structural changes.
Background: Studies have consistently associated parental separation with lower socioeconomic achievement. However, the underlying causes remain unclear, possibly due to self-selection or continued instability in the family environment.
Method: This study employs structural causal modeling and Bayesian logistic regression to investigate the causal effect of childhood family structural changes on adult educational attainment using Finnish full-population register data (n = 272,962), focusing on cohorts born in 1987–1991 and their family members.
Results: A substantial portion of the 17-percentage-point disadvantage of children from separated families was due to the selection of families into separation and further structural changes. Separation effects followed a U-shaped pattern, with the largest negative effects among families in the middle of the educational gradient. Stepparents did not exert positive or negative effects, except for highly educated parents’ children benefiting from stable stepfathers. Recurring family instability had the largest negative impact.
Conclusion: The curvilinear relationship between parental and child education may partly explain the mixed results of previous studies.
Implications: Given that recurring family instability was shown to be the most detrimental to children’s educational attainment, low-threshold guidance and support to aspiring and existing blended families might have the largest impacts on children of separated parents.

articles.title: High-resolution projections of vulnerable populations under the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways
articles.authors: Christina Draeger; Martino Tran; Ben Mumford
articles.abstract: Vulnerable populations are disproportionately affected by climate change and face limited adaptive resources, yet remain largely overlooked in regional planning and decision-making. Future population composition and the spatial distribution of vulnerability shape both mitigation strategies, which reduce the magnitude of climate change, and adaptation strategies, which reduce its impacts, with direct implications for policy interventions and planning. This study provides high-resolution (1 km) population projections for Canada until 2100 across age, sex, ethnicity, income, and education under the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways. Using a novel deep learning–based population dynamics model that simultaneously predicts full population compositions and incorporates macroeconomic, governance, and land surface variables, we examine demographic shifts at national, regional, and local scales. Results reveal substantial variability across scenarios: the proportion of seniors rises most in low-mitigation-challenge scenarios, particularly along lower-emission pathways, while the share of children and youth increases most in scenarios with high mitigation challenges. Ethnic diversity expands when mitigation challenges are low but decreases or stagnates when they are high. Income and education distributions are most sensitive to adaptation challenges, with the strongest polarization of socioeconomic groups into a three-class society and the largest increase in the share of individuals without a diploma occurring when both adaptation and mitigation challenges are high, highlighting the need for high-resolution, disaggregated demographic projections to guide targeted and equitable interventions for vulnerable communities.

articles.title: Synthesizing Tabular Microdata with Gaussian Copulas: The rsdv R Package
articles.authors: Kailas Venkitasubramanian
articles.abstract:
Administrative records, survey microdata, and clinical research data carry
  identifying detail that data-governance procedures restrict from open
  release. Synthetic data — artificial rows that preserve the distributional
  structure of a real dataset without releasing any single observation — is
  now widely used as a substitute when the underlying records cannot be
  shared. The R ecosystem has had good options
  for parts of this workflow, but no native implementation of the copula-based
  joint-modelling design that the Python Synthetic Data Vault (SDV) library
  popularised. The `rsdv` package fills that gap: it fits a Gaussian copula
  jointly over numerical, categorical, and boolean columns, supports
  conditional sampling and declarative row-level constraints, and ships three
  evaluation reports (quality, structural validity, and privacy) modelled on
  SDMetrics' two-property hierarchy. I describe the package's design,
  benchmark it against the most widely used R alternatives (`synthpop` and
  `arf`) on the UCI Adult Income data, report quality scores as a function
  of training-set size, and quantify attribute-disclosure risk across four
  threat models. `synthpop` produces the highest marginal fidelity on this
  dataset; `arf` is fast and competitive; `rsdv` is comparable to `synthpop`
  on privacy and uniquely brings the SDV-style integrated reporting
  pipeline to R. The package is on CRAN, has 200+ tests, three vignettes,
  and a reproducible replication archive accompanies this paper.

articles.title: Beyond the Takeover: Youth Gatherings, Urban Public Space, and the Racialization of Disorder in Chicago
articles.authors: Almethia C. Franklin
articles.abstract:
In recent years, Chicago has witnessed a significant rise in large-scale youth gatherings in high-visibility public spaces. Labeled "teen takeovers" in dominant media and policy discourse, these events are almost exclusively framed as public safety threats requiring law enforcement response. This paper argues that this framing is both analytically insufficient and politically consequential. Drawing on preliminary media and news analysis, an emerging qualitative research agenda, and a multi-disciplinary theoretical framework, this paper reframes youth gatherings as social phenomena that reveal fundamental dynamics of race, space, territorial boundary, and urban belonging. We identify three dimensions that existing scholarship has not yet systematically examined: (1) the role of social media and digital technology in organizing, escalating, and documenting these events; (2) the apparent dissolution of hyperlocal territorial boundaries historically maintained by street organizations in Chicago; and (3) the variation in form, activity, and framing of analogous youth gatherings across U.S. cities. We situate these dimensions within a five-part theoretical architecture drawing on moral panic theory, critical criminology, spatial theory, Black cultural studies, and the sociology of punishment. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for empirical investigation and discussing the implications of this work for policy and community response.

Keywords: youth gatherings, teen takeovers, urban public space, moral panic, territorial boundaries, Chicago, racialization, social media, cultural criminology

articles.title: Your Concerns Have Been Noted: Constituent Influence and Support for Housing
articles.authors: Alexandre Rivard
articles.abstract:
Opposition to new housing development is widespread and neighbourhood defenders—
proximate residents who mobilize against proposed projects—are a well-documented
feature of local planning politics. Yet we know relatively little about how political
elites process these place-based claims. Using a conjoint experiment embedded in the
2026 Canadian Municipal Barometer, I examine the extent to which city councilors
weight the preferences of constituents based on their proximity to a proposed housing
development. The results confirm that councilors are more responsive to proximate con
stituents. This proximity effect is amplified by neighbourhood tenure and is stronger
for denser development types. Contrary to expectations, neither the councilor’s ideol
ogy, density preferences, electoral district type, nor acclamation status conditions this
responsiveness—suggesting that localism is a structural feature of local representation
rather than an ideological predisposition. Taken together, the findings suggest that
place-protective claims find a receptive audience among political elites.

articles.title: Social capital is associated with both risk-taking and risk-avoidance in rural Bangladesh
articles.authors: Joon Hwang; Nurul Alam; Mary K Shenk
articles.abstract: Throughout human evolutionary history, individuals have faced two fundamental challenges under uncertainty: deciding whether to take risks and managing risks through cooperation. While both risk-taking and social risk management have been widely studied, less attention has been given to how these two processes are linked—specifically, how risk-taking itself may be shaped by social networks. We test the "social capital buffer" hypothesis, which posits that greater social connectedness promotes risk-taking by buffering against negative outcomes. Analyzing social networks and risk preferences among 140 individuals in rural Bangladesh whose livelihoods range from farming to wage labor and small-scale trade, we identify distinct pathways through which social capital influences risk preference. Highly clustered individuals in financial support networks exhibit greater risk preference, suggesting that clustering facilitates risk-taking by ensuring resource circulation within a tight-knit group. In contrast, individuals with more support-receiving ties in material support networks are more risk-averse, indicating that material support functions as informal social insurance reducing reliance on risky decisions. Finally, reciprocity in material support networks is associated with risk-taking only among wealthier individuals, suggesting interactions between social and economic capital underlying variation in risk preference. These findings reveal that social capital may not uniformly promote or constrain risk-taking but serves distinct adaptive functions based on network structure, economic conditions, and resource types, balancing risk-taking and risk-avoidance to help individuals successfully navigate uncertainty.

articles.title: From Status Symbol to Polysemous Sign: Interpreting the Meaning of Artificially Extended Fingernails
articles.authors: Jürgen Beyer; Lea Kirn
articles.abstract: The study analyses how German-language press coverage interprets the trend toward artificially elongated fingernails and relates it to Veblen’s concept of conspicuous leisure. Drawing on a qualitative-interpretive analysis of 45 newspaper articles, it reconstructs eight interpretive frames that locate long nails at the intersection of status display, consumer aesthetics, feminist gesture, self-care ritual, migrant-coded service economies, and scenes of cultural appropriation. The press does invoke Veblen’s reading but simultaneously marks it as anachronistic, replacing univocal status semantics with a polysemous structure that links long nails to heterogeneous problem constellations (capitalist innovation, digital visibility, gender politics, self-care). This polysemy becomes the driving force of the trend’s appeal, turning a marginal bodily detail into a medium through which the contradictory imperatives of late-modern subjectivation are materially negotiated.

articles.title: State Greenwashing: Who Watches the Green-Watcher?
articles.authors: Julien O. Beaulieu; Malaïka Jacques-Bérubé; Tiffanie Chan
articles.abstract: State greenwashing has received little academic attention despite the number of state greenwashing accusations in media and civil society discourse. Existing scholarship overwhelmingly focuses on corporate greenwashing, leaving the state context largely unexamined. This is a gap that is increasingly timely to address, as some governments are considering reducing their climate ambition while seeking to preserve a climate-friendly image. This article addresses that gap. We use an iterative and deductive, theory-informed approach that draws on the academic literature on greenwashing, political communication, climate governance and on climate litigation cases to propose a novel typology of state greenwashing. We identify two new tactics that are particularly salient in the state context and absent from corporate-focused greenwashing typologies. The first, amplification, occurs when state actors relay or reproduce deceptive environmental claims originating from corporate sources through official government communication channels. The second, greenlighting, involves the formal endorsement and legitimisation of contested environmental claims by the state in its capacity as regulator and policymaker. Our analysis reveals that greenlighting is more likely to generate legal challenges than amplification. We find that the legal frameworks typically mobilised against corporate greenwashing are ill-suited for the state context, leading stakeholders to mobilize frameworks spanning procedural, administrative, human rights, and environmental law. Determining whether a state claim constitutes greenwashing involves a degree of subjectivity, especially where shifting political priorities, evolving circumstances, or unforeseen constraints lead to undelivered climate commitments, or where disagreement exists regarding scientific interpretation. We conclude by identifying three priority areas for policy intervention: strengthening state reporting obligations, enhancing the public’s capacity to critically evaluate state environmental claims, and empowering independent knowledge brokers.

articles.title: Scarcity, Strategy, and the Racialized Politics of Refugee Admission in the Global North
articles.authors: Andrew Rosenberg
articles.abstract: Why do some refugee "crises'' elicit generosity while others provoke restriction, particularly in the Global North? In 2015, European governments responded to Syrian arrivals with increasingly restrictive policies, yet the same states were welcoming toward Ukrainians in 2022. I develop a formal model in which governments weigh economic scarcity, the racialized identity of arrivals, and foreign-policy salience. The model predicts that scarcity amplifies restriction toward non-White groups but can weaken or even reverse restrictiveness toward White groups when crises are geopolitically salient. I test these propositions using a country-year panel of asylum-policy admission and entry rules across Global North states. The results show that scarcity heightens backlash against non-White arrivals but reduces restrictiveness toward White arrivals when foreign-policy considerations are salient. Together, the findings demonstrate how race, economic conditions, and foreign policy interact to structure the politics of protection in the Global North.

articles.title: Beyond task performance: morphological branching as a diagnostic metric for polish inflectional language models
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
Subword tokenization has become a standard preprocessing step in transformer-based language models, yet its consequences for inflectional languages remain insufficiently examined. Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) optimizes segmentation for frequency and compression rather than morphological structure, which may fragment forms belonging to the same inflectional paradigm into unrelated token sequences before they reach the encoder. This paper introduces morphological branching as a diagnostic approach for evaluating whether language models preserve paradigmatic coherence in Polish, a morphologically rich language.
We test three models from the Bielik family — Bielik-4.5B-v3.0-Instruct, Bielik-7B-v0.1-Instruct, and Bielik-11B-v2.3-Instruct — on a purpose-built benchmark of 26 morphological elicitation tasks. The benchmark probes noun-adjective agreement, verb paradigm production, suppletive motion verbs, and adverb inflection traps. Model responses are compared with a seven-person native speaker baseline. The study identifies three categories of morphological failure: paradigm error, syncretism error, and category error, with particular attention to cross-paradigm substitution.
The central finding is that two of the three models produced szłam, a past-tense form of iść (‘to go’, directional), in response to a prompt requiring a form of chodzić (‘to go’, habitual). This error crosses a lexeme boundary and suggests that the model does not reliably maintain the distinction between paradigmatically related but morphologically distinct motion verbs. Adverb trap items produced a different pattern: models sometimes generated inflected adjectival forms instead of recognizing that Polish adverbs are uninflectable. Model accuracy increased with parameter size, from 61% for Bielik-4.5B to 73% for Bielik-11B, but all models remained below the human baseline of 87%.
These results suggest that task-based benchmarks alone are insufficient for evaluating language models in inflectional languages. A model may perform well on downstream tasks while still failing to preserve the internal structure of paradigms. The paper argues that morphological paradigm coherence should be treated as an independent dimension of language model evaluation, especially for languages in which grammatical meaning is encoded through inflectional form rather than word order.

Translation note.This preprint is based on a text originally drafted in Polish and subsequently translated into English by the author. Minor editorial and terminological adjustments were made to improve clarity for an international readership.

articles.title: A Cross-National Analysis of Antifeminist Sentiments in Europe:  Age Patterns and Gender Gaps
articles.authors: Richard Nennstiel; Christina Siegert
articles.abstract: Recent research highlights growing gender divides among younger individuals in attitudes toward gender equality and feminism, often suggesting that young men exhibit particularly high levels of modern antifeminism. This study investigates the prevalence of modern antifeminist sentiment across Europe, focusing on how gender differences vary across age groups, and contrasts these patterns with traditional antifeminism. Using Eurobarometer data from 2024, we operationalize modern antifeminism as agreement that "feminism has gone too far" and traditional antifeminism as endorsement of traditional gender roles. Results show these dimensions operate distinctly. While traditional antifeminism is consistently higher among older respondents, modern antifeminism features a wide gender gap specifically within younger age groups. Crucially, this gap is driven by young women’s exceptionally low agreement, rather than unusually high levels among young men. Furthermore, young men represent the most antifeminist demographic in only a minority of the 27 investigated countries.

articles.title: Acceptance of transport policies:  A factorial survey experiment in  the German Longitudinal  Environmental Study (GLEN)
articles.authors: Christiane Bozoyan; Claudia Schmiedeberg; Henning Best
articles.abstract: Due to the significant negative externalities associated with the transport sector, policies that effectively address these issues and guide the transition to a low-carbon transport system are required. In order to gain insight into the level of public support for a number of transport policy interventions currently under debate in Germany, a factorial survey experiment on interventions to promote the transition from combustion engine vehicles to battery electric vehicles (BEVs) was conducted as part of the GLEN starter survey in January/February 2025. This report documents the experiment.

articles.title: Food Insecurity and the Accumulation of Material  Hardship: Evidence from the CPS, SIPP, and PSID
articles.authors: Thomas O'Rourke
articles.abstract: Food insecurity is widely used to identify vulnerable households, yet the literature remains divided over what exactly it captures. One perspective treats food insecurity as a proxy for economic disadvantage—a view supported by cross-sectional associations but complicated by recent causal evidence. A second perspective contends that food insecurity reflects subjective perceptions of hardship rather than objective economic deprivation, but empirical evidence on this hypothesis is limited. A third perspective emphasizes that food insecurity primarily depends on household experiences of other forms of hardship. Using longitudinal data from three nationally representative surveys, this study adjudicates between these perspectives by examining how food insecurity changes within households as economic resources, subjective wellbeing, and material hardship change over time. I find that food insecurity is highly stable within households over time and is only weakly responsive to changes in economic resources and subjective wellbeing. By contrast, food insecurity increases when households experience other direct forms of hardship, including worsening housing conditions and greater difficulty paying bills. These findings challenge the idea that households substitute one form of hardship for another when navigating scarcity. Instead, food insecurity appears to coincide with other forms of material hardship: households experiencing hardship in one domain are often exposed to hardship in others.

articles.title: Heterogeneous Effects of Climate, Narco-Trafficking, and Violence on Central American Migration to the United States
articles.authors: Fabien Cottier; David Wrathall; Jennifer A. Devine; Elise Arellano-Thompson; Rebecca Gustine; David Leblang; Nicholas Magliocca; Jennifer Nakamura; Elizabeth Tellman; Alex de Sherbinin
articles.abstract: Apprehensions at the US Southern Border have steadily increased over the past decade, with narratives stressing the impact of institutional instability, gang violence, economic crises, and climate change. Here, we empirically investigate how two important features of the political and security context in Guatemala and Honduras interact with climate variability to shape irregular migration to the United States: criminal violence and drug trafficking organizations (DTOs). Using quantitative modeling and theory building, this paper examines how temperature and precipitation deviations interact with the presence of narco-trafficking organizations and homicide violence at the municipio level to amplify or attenuate apprehensions along the US Southwest Border. Our results reveal important heterogeneities across countries, regarding both the impact of environmental drivers on apprehensions, and the conditional effects of violence and organized crime. Specifically, we find that violence and narco-trafficking activities are associated with a weaker relationship between high temperatures and apprehensions of migrants from Guatemala but find no evidence for similar processes in Honduras.
