Drawing on cognitive dissonance theory, this study examines climate inaction from a behavior-to-attitude perspective, proposing that limited action may trigger self-justifying processes that distance individuals from climate risks and shape climate-related information engagement. A nationwide online survey of 500 Malaysian adults was analysed using structural equation modelling. Results broadly supported the proposed model: climate inaction was associated with moral disengagement, greater psychological distance, lower climate concern, and higher climate skepticism, while climate information seeking played a complex role in these attitudinal outcomes. The findings suggest that climate inaction may actively sustain future disengagement by reshaping psychological and communicative responses to climate change.

articles.title: Understanding Human-Technology Interaction: Models, Ethics, and the Future of Control
articles.authors: Kristina Sabrina Weißmüller; Pascal König
articles.abstract: Drawing on the latest interdisciplinary insights on human-technology interaction, this conceptual chapter explains key perspectives and frameworks of how people adopt, interact with, and experience technology. After introducing core ethical aspects of technology, the chapter takes a critical stance on the promises and pitfalls of disruptive technological advancement—including but not limited to AI. Combining organizational, socio-psychological, and ethical perspectives on human-technology interaction, fundamental challenges of human control over digital technologies—which are not only tools but also agents and infrastructures—are explained. Based on this literature review, the chapter highlights and explains important blind spots within the commonly used models of human-technology interaction and discusses unresolved issues of user-technology value alignment, accountability, and control.

articles.title: The Moral Question: Norms and Anti-System Politics
articles.authors: Daniel A. N. Goldstein; Tore Wig
articles.abstract: Do moral norms influence citizens' tolerance of anti-system behavior by politicians? Although moral norms are often invoked as guardrails, their measurement and causal influence in politics remain understudied. We theorize the link between moral norms, personal judgments, and attitudinal consequences and test our theory in a pre-registered survey experiment with financial incentives. Respondents faced moral trade-offs concerning personal scandals, political violence, corruption, and anti-democratic actions. Using a second-order approach to elicit beliefs about what others consider morally wrong, we find that norms against democratic violations are descriptively weaker than norms against scandal and corruption; both lag behind norms against violence. Causal evidence shows that norms primarily shift judgments when respondents face large discrepancies from their initial perceived norm and have strong conformity motives. However, this largely does not translate into attitudinal shifts. The findings shed light on the concept of moral norms and detail their consequences and limitations for politics.

articles.title: The Disappearance of the Social?  A Conceptual Critique of the Para- and Post-Social Media Debate
articles.authors: Miira Hill
articles.abstract: Recent contributions by danah boyd (2026) and Törnberg & Rogers (2026) have argued that social media platforms have undergone a fundamental transformation: what once enabled reciprocal social interaction has become a landscape of parasocial observation, algorithmic broadcasting, and post-social fragmentation. This essay takes these empirical diagnoses seriously while subjecting their conceptual foundations to critical scrutiny. It argues that both contributions operate with an implicitly normative and analytically underdetermined concept of sociality, one that reduces a theoretically complex category to a historically particular manifestation. Drawing on the sociology of knowledge (Berger & Luckmann), communicative constructivism (Knoblauch, Keller), actor-network theory (Latour), feminist materialism (Haraway), and the concept of object-centred sociality (Knorr Cetina), the essay argues that sociality was never independent of its material and technical mediations, and that the relationship to objects, algorithms, and non-human actors is itself a form of sociality rather than its absence. A further objection is epistemological: ‘social media’ was never a scientifically precise category but an ethnocategory, an industry term and journalistic shorthand whose normative charge has been unreflectively adopted as an analytical framework. The essay concludes that the current debate diagnoses less the end of ‘social media’ than the crisis of a never-sufficiently-defined scientific category, and calls for a more systematic engagement with theoretical resources, available both within and beyond communication research, that may help to specify what the social is, and where it resides in contemporary digital environments.

articles.title: Simplifying Sociology: When Complex Writing Occurs, Where It Matters, and How It Can Be Improved
articles.authors: Carsten Schwemmer; Sebastian Block; Sandra Jaworeck; Manuel Holz; Philipp Spielbauer
articles.abstract: Clarity and precision are vital in science communication, yet sociology is often criticized for using overly complex language. In this study, we examine the complexity of sociological writing using an original dataset of 1,700 full-text articles from three major German journals. We validate a composite measure to investigate complexity over time and across methodological orientations, which past work has shown to follow different research conventions. We find that peak textual complexity in sociological articles increases over time and is systematically related to methodological choices. Next, we examine the relationship between complexity and citation-based impact. In contrast with findings from other disciplines, our results provide no evidence for complex writing to either harm or boost the number of citations per article. Finally, we demonstrate a simple workflow that uses our composite measure alongside generative artificial intelligence. This approach can identify highly complex passages and support targeted revision, thereby contributing to clearer communication and accessibility in academia and beyond.

articles.title: These Four First: A Robust Endogenous Baseline for Relational Event Sequences
articles.authors: C. Ben Gibson
articles.abstract: Which endogenous interaction mechanisms belong in a baseline relational event model? We test for recurring endogenous patterns in 217 empirical interaction sequences spanning ten social domains, evaluating a twelve-term candidate set by forward AIC reduction. Four terms appear in the top-four selection path in over 60% of domains — interaction-order constraints (IOviolation, 94%), prior sender initiation (SndInitiation, 88%), rank-recency of the most recent reverse-dyad event (RecRecencyRank, 65%), and dyadic repetition (Repetition, 61%) — with substantially diminishing returns at step 4. Together they re- cover approximately 97% of the maximum AIC improvement achievable within this candi- date set and represent three distinct behavioral mechanisms: sequential ordering, dyadic persistence, and reverse-dyad recency. Notably absent from this baseline are imitation (preferential attachment driven by others’ targeting behavior) and triadic closure terms, despite their prominent roles in the network literature; both are absorbed by forward-dyad history once SndInitiation and Repetition enter the model. IOviolation’s dominance as the most consistently selected term reflects the near-universal importance of participation- shift norms across interaction settings, even those that are not conversational. The step-2 composition diverges ecologically: in high-visibility settings RecRecencyRank competes closely with IOviolation at step 2 (38% vs. 60%), while in low-visibility settings IOviola- tion dominates step 2 in 79% of domains. These four terms are not a sufficient model — institutional context, status, demographic attributes, and additional endogenous mech- anisms all shape interaction — but they are a consistent baseline that researchers can include with confidence before introducing substantive covariates. The cross-domain sta- bility of this four-term core — spanning private messaging, classroom interaction, and gang violence — hints at a compact set of dyadic mechanisms that may constitute near- universal building blocks of directed social interaction.

articles.title: High Agreement, Different Stories: How LLM Classifiers Reshape Demographic Patterns in Survey Data
articles.authors: Chris Soria
articles.abstract:
What we learn from open-ended survey data depends on who—or what—does the coding. Large Language Models (LLMs) promise to democratize qualitative analysis, but do high agreement rates translate into equivalent thematic findings? This study compares eight LLMs to human annotators on a multilabel coding task using 3,200 responses from the UC Berkeley Social Networks Study, comprising over 19,000 coding decisions. Although LLM-human reliability does not match human-human reliability overall, LLMs approach human performance on simpler tasks and can serve as useful additional coders for generating consensus labels. Compared to a gold-standard human consensus, models achieve 82–97% per-category agreement, but macro F1 is lower and response-level similarity is lower still: even the best model reproduces the full human label set for fewer than 60% of responses. Yet high agreement masks thematic divergence. Models systematically over-identify themes, assigning 67% more categories per response, especially for categories requiring greater interpretive judgment.

Models also show lower agreement for some demographic groups. These gaps are partly explained by response characteristics such as length, clarity, and atypicality, and some persist after controls, with implications for studies of populations whose response styles diverge from the corpus average. At the sample level, models largely preserve the overall thematic narrative: human and model category rankings correlate strongly (pooled Spearman's ρ=0.75), and top-performing models achieve approximately 80% directional agreement on demographic patterns. Concrete behavioral questions, such as reasons for moving or strategies for making friends, show especially strong alignment. Yet systematic over-classification can still shift narratives about how specific groups behave, leading researchers to report patterns that the human gold standard does not support.

articles.title: Social Culture in the Era of Generative AI: Structural Transformation, Cognitive Reshaping, and Future Perspectives
articles.authors: Jincheng Zhang
articles.abstract: The rapid development of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) is profoundly reshaping the fundamental structure of human society and culture. From knowledge production methods to daily communication patterns, from cultural content creation to value formation mechanisms, AI is transforming from a "tool-based technology" into a "cultural force." This paper systematically analyzes the impact mechanism of generative AI on contemporary social culture from four dimensions: social structure, cultural production, identity recognition, and ethical order, and further explores the cognitive reshaping and future development trends it has triggered. The study argues that generative AI not only changes the efficiency of cultural dissemination but also restructures the relationship between "humans, technology, and culture," enabling social culture to enter a new stage of "co-generation."

articles.title: A demographic theory of similarity-biased social learning
articles.authors: Alejandro Pérez Velilla; Paul E. Smaldino
articles.abstract: We develop a demographic theory of similarity-biased social learning that formalizes our understanding of when and why individuals should preferentially copy others that look or act like them. We build an evolutionary model in which individuals can either learn on their own or copy others from a demonstrator pool that contains varying proportions of in-group and out-group members, and where group tags can be more or less informative about local knowledge. We find that where social learning becomes common, selection favors copying biases that track the direction of informational advantage---toward the group that tends to be better adapted to local conditions, including an anti-similarity bias when tags are negatively associated with local correctness (as may be the case for some immigrant communities). We also find conditions in which a similarity bias can stabilize social learning when such learners are already common, but not when they are rare, with implications for the role of group identities in cultural evolution.  We discuss implications for understanding parochialism as risk aversion, majority-minority dynamics, the sociology of immigration, and the lasting impacts of colonialism.
